math.title: The math that decides pots
math.subtitle: Pot odds, equity and the rule of 2 and 4, no calculator needed. The foundation that turns a guess into a decision.
math.intro_p1: A lot of players run away from poker math thinking it takes a genius. It doesn't. The calculation that decides most pots fits in your head, no calculator needed, and it's what separates a guess from a decision. When someone bets and you have to choose between calling or folding, there's a number that tells you whether calling is profitable in the long run. Learning to estimate that number is the most profitable step a beginner can take.
math.intro_p2: In this lesson you'll learn three simple tools: pot odds (the price of the call), equity (your chance of winning) and the rule of 2 and 4 (a shortcut to estimate the equity of your draws). In the end, you'll put it all into a single question: 'does my chance of winning pay the price they're charging me?'.
math.intro_note: Every time you face a bet with a draw (flush, straight) or a medium hand, this calculation decides. It's the most common situation in poker, and the one that most separates the players who win from the ones who bleed chips over the long run.
math.obj1: Calculate pot odds in your head: the price of a call.
math.obj2: Estimate your equity by counting outs with the rule of 2 and 4.
math.obj3: Decide whether to call or fold by comparing equity with pot odds.
math.obj4: Understand EV: why the correct decision is always the one with the highest value over the long run.
math.fund_p1: Equity is your chance of winning the pot, as a percentage. If you have 40% equity, in a world where the same situation happened 100 times, you'd win 40 of them. An out is a card that hasn't come yet and that improves your hand to probably the best one: a flush draw has 9 outs (the 9 remaining cards of the suit). Counting outs is how you estimate the equity of a draw.
math.fund_p2: Pot odds is the price of the call: how much you have to pay relative to what you can win. If the pot has 100 and the villain bets, you pay an amount to try to take a bigger pot. The smaller the price relative to the pot, the cheaper it is to continue, and the less equity you need. The poker decision becomes a comparison: is your equity greater than the price? If yes, call. If not, fold.
math.c1_title: Chapter 1: pot odds, the price of the call
math.c1_p1: The math is a simple division: how much you pay divided by the total pot after your call. Example: the pot has 100, the villain bets 50. To call, you put in 50. The total pot after your call will be 100 + 50 + 50 = 200. So you pay 50 to win 200: 50 / 200 = 25%. That's your price: you need at least 25% equity for the call to break even over the long run.
math.c1_p2: Interpretation: 25% means that, if you win 1 out of every 4 times, you're already not losing money. Winning more than that, you profit. That's why a small bet is easy to call (the price is low) and an overbet is expensive (the price goes up): the bigger the bet relative to the pot, the more equity your hand needs.
math.c1_key: Pot odds = amount to call / total pot after the call. It's the MINIMUM equity your hand needs for the call to be worth it.
math.c2_title: Chapter 2: outs and the rule of 2 and 4
math.c2_p1: To know whether your equity beats the price, you need to estimate it. The shortcut is the rule of 2 and 4. Count your outs and: on the flop, if you're going to see the next two cards (turn and river), multiply by 4; if you'll only see the next card, multiply by 2. The result is your approximate equity, as a percentage. Fast, no calculator, and close enough to decide.
math.c2_cap: You have A-K of hearts and the flop brings two hearts (Q-7). There are 9 hearts left in the deck: those are 9 outs to the best flush. By the rule of 4: 9 x 4 = 36% equity by the river.
math.c2_p2: In the example on the table, your flush draw has 9 outs. By the rule of 4 (you'll still see turn and river), the equity is about 9 x 4 = 36%. Keep that number: in the next step, you'll compare it with the price to decide. A flush draw on the flop has roughly a 36% chance to complete, a number worth memorizing.
math.c2_tip: Common flop draws (rule of 4): flush = ~36% (9 outs), open-ended straight = ~32% (8 outs), gutshot = ~16% (4 outs). On the turn (rule of 2), divide by two, roughly.
math.c3_title: Chapter 3: putting it together, call or fold
math.c3_p1: Now the decision is a direct comparison. You have your equity (from the rule of 2 and 4) and your price (from the pot odds). The rule is simple: if your equity is greater than the price, call; if it's less, fold. In the example, the flush draw has ~36% equity and the price was 25%: 36% is greater than 25%, so calling is profitable over the long run. If the price were 40% (a big bet), then 36% wouldn't pay and the right move would be to fold.
math.c3_key: Call when your equity is greater than the pot odds. Fold when it's less. It's this comparison, not the guess, that decides the pot.
math.c4_title: Chapter 4: EV, the value of the decision
math.c4_p1: All of this serves a bigger idea: EV (expected value). EV is how much a decision wins or loses, on average, if you repeated it many times. The correct decision is always the one with the highest EV. When your equity beats the price, the call has positive EV (it wins money over the long run); when it doesn't, the call has negative EV (it loses). You don't control which card comes, but you control whether you make positive-EV decisions. Do that enough and the result shows up.
math.c4_coach: Stop thinking 'did I win or lose this pot' and start thinking 'was my decision positive EV?'. You can make the right decision and lose the hand; over the long run, it's the decision that pays the bills, not the result of a single hand.
math.c5_title: Chapter 5: implied odds, one step beyond
math.c5_p1: Sometimes you call with equity a bit below the price, and it's still profitable. Why? Because of implied odds: the extra money you expect to win on the next streets when you complete your hand. If you have a flush draw and you know that, when you hit it, you'll get more chips from the villain, that future gain makes up for calling a little 'expensive' now. Careful: implied odds only count when there are stacks to win later and the villain tends to pay; don't use this as an excuse to call everything.
math.c5_curio: The opposite also exists: reverse implied odds, when completing your hand can still make you lose a bigger pot (for example, you make the smaller flush and the villain makes the bigger one). That's why draws to the best hand, the nut draws, are worth more than dominated draws.
math.ex_p1: Situation: you have A-K of hearts on the button. The flop comes Q-7-2 with two hearts, so you have a draw to the best flush: 9 outs. The villain bets 5 into a pot of 10. Call or fold? First the price: you pay 5 into a total pot of 10 + 5 + 5 = 20, that is 5 / 20 = 25%. Then the equity: 9 outs by the rule of 4 give ~36%.
math.ex_p2: Correct decision: call. Your equity (~36%) is greater than the price (25%), so calling has positive EV, even if you lose this specific hand. And there are still implied odds: if the flush completes, you'll probably win more chips. Incorrect decision: folding 'because I have nothing yet'. That ignores the draw's equity and throws away a profitable call. In poker, you pay for the chance to win, not for the hand you already have.
math.cmp_r1_rec: Calls or folds 'on feel', without counting anything.
math.cmp_r1_reg: Knows pot odds, but sometimes miscounts outs.
math.cmp_r1_pro: Compares equity and price almost instantly, in their head.
math.cmp_r2_rec: On the result of the pot ('did I win or lose').
math.cmp_r2_reg: Halfway there, still rattled by a bad beat.
math.cmp_r2_pro: On the EV of the decision; the result of one hand doesn't change the line.
math.cmp_r3_reg: Calls draws, but ignores implied and reverse odds.
math.cmp_r3_pro: Values nut draws and avoids dominated draws.
math.e1_m: Calling 'on feel', without calculating the price.
math.e1_why: Without pot odds, you call bets that are too expensive and lose over the long run.
math.e1_fix: Do the division: call / total pot. Compare it with your equity.
math.e2_m: Miscounting outs (counting cards that don't give you the best hand).
math.e2_why: Inflated outs give false equity and lead to bad calls.
math.e2_fix: Count only the outs that make the winning hand; watch out for dominated draws.
math.e3_m: Using the rule of 4 when you'll only see one card.
math.e3_why: If you won't see both cards, the 4 doubles your real equity.
math.e3_fix: Two cards to come: x4. Only the next one: x2.
math.e4_m: Folding strong draws at a cheap price.
math.e4_why: A flush draw (~36%) easily pays a price of 25%; folding throws away EV.
math.e4_fix: Compare: if the equity beats the price, call.
math.e5_why: Losing a hand doesn't mean the decision was wrong.
math.e5_fix: Judge the decision by its EV, not by the card that came.
math.e6_m: Abusing implied odds to justify any call.
math.e6_why: Without stacks to win later, the future gain doesn't exist.
math.e6_fix: Only count implied odds when there are chips and a villain who tends to pay.
math.chk1: What's the price? (amount to call / total pot after the call)
math.chk2: How many outs do I really have (only the ones that make the winning hand)?
math.chk3: What's my equity? (outs x 4 on the flop with two cards to come, x 2 with one)
math.chk4: Is my equity greater than the price? If yes, call; if not, fold.
math.chk5: Are there real implied odds (stack and a villain who pays) to adjust the decision?
math.drill_sub: Exercises on pot odds, outs and call or fold, generated from your own spots. Each correct answer earns XP.
math.t1: Pot odds = call / total pot after the call: the minimum equity you need.
math.t2: Estimate equity by counting outs: x4 on the flop (two cards), x2 (one card).
math.t3: Call when your equity is greater than the price; fold when it's less.
math.t4: The correct decision is the one with the highest EV; judge the decision, not the result of the hand.
math.g1_def: Your chance of winning the pot, as a percentage.
math.g2_def: A card that hasn't come yet and improves your hand to probably the best one.
math.g3_def: The price of the call: the amount to pay divided by the total pot after the call.
math.g4_def: A shortcut to estimate equity: outs x4 on the flop (two cards), x2 when one card is left.
math.g5_def: Expected value: how much a decision wins or loses, on average, over the long run.
math.g6_def: The extra money you expect to win on the next streets when you complete your hand.

math.gl1: Flags calls and folds where your equity didn't match the price (pot odds).
math.gl2: Marks the decisions with lost EV and how much each one cost.
math.gl3: Shows the profitable call indicator when you folded a hand that had the odds.

math_int.title: Equity in practice: matchups, outs and range
math_int.subtitle: You already know the rule of 2 and 4. Now sharpen your equity estimate: the classic matchups, combining and discounting outs, and calculating equity against a whole range.
math_int.intro_p1: In the Math lesson you learned to estimate equity by counting outs with the rule of 2 and 4, and to compare it with the pot odds. That solves most calls. But there are situations where the rough estimate is off: matchups between made hands (a pair against high cards), combined draws, outs that aren't 'clean', and the difference between calculating against ONE hand and against a whole RANGE. This lesson sharpens your equity reading for those cases.
math_int.intro_p2: Nothing here is hard math; it's a few numbers to memorize and a few adjustments so you don't fool yourself. You'll memorize the classic matchups (the ones that show up all the time in an all-in), learn to add up the outs of combined draws, to discount 'dirty' outs, and to think about equity against the villain's range, not against a single imagined hand.
math_int.intro_note: This lesson assumes you already master pot odds, outs and the rule of 2 and 4 (the Math lesson). If you don't yet, do that one first; here we go one step beyond.
math_int.obj1: Memorize the classic matchups of equity (coinflip, pair vs pair, dominated).
math_int.obj2: Add up the outs of combined draws (flush + straight).
math_int.obj3: Discount dirty outs that give the villain a better hand.
math_int.obj4: Estimate equity against a range, not against a single hand.
math_int.fund_p1: Two refinements guide this lesson. First: equity against a hand is not the same as equity against a range. You almost never know the villain's exact hand; you know the set of hands they can have. Your real equity is the average against all of them, and it's usually different from what you imagine when you look only at the hand that scares you most. Second: not every out is clean. A dirty out is a card that improves your hand, but gives the villain an even better one.
math_int.fund_p2: Beyond that, it pays to memorize a few matchups that show up all the time, mainly in preflop all-ins. Knowing by heart that a pair against two high cards is roughly a coinflip, or that a big pair crushes a small pair, saves you from calculating on the spot and avoids costly mistakes. Round numbers, memorized, are worth more than a perfect calculation done with a shaking hand.
math_int.c1_p1: Some matchups come up so often that it pays to know the equity by heart. The table below shows the most common ones, from the favorite's point of view. Notice the pattern: a pair against two high cards is almost a coinflip (that's why 'coinflip'); a bigger pair against a smaller one is a beating; and a dominated hand (same Ace, worse kicker) is well behind. Memorizing these four already covers most of the preflop all-ins you'll face.
math_int.tbl_r1_a: Pair vs 2 high cards (88 vs AK)
math_int.tbl_r2_a: Bigger pair vs smaller pair (QQ vs 77)
math_int.c1_key: Coinflip ~55/45, pair vs pair ~80/20, dominated ~70/30, overpair vs draw ~55/45. With these four you solve almost any all-in in your head.
math_int.c2_p1: When you have two draws at the same time, the outs add up, and your equity shoots up. In the scene, you have J-T of hearts on a 9-8-2 flop with two hearts: it's a flush draw (9 outs) PLUS an open-ended straight draw (8 more outs, a Q or 7). Discounting the cards that count twice, you're left with about 15 outs. By the rule of 4, that's about 60% equity: you're even a favorite against many made hands. Combined draws are monsters disguised as 'I've got nothing'.
math_int.c2_cap: J-T of hearts on a 9h-8s-2h flop: flush draw (9 outs) + open-ended straight (8 outs) = about 15 outs. A very strong semi-bluff.
math_int.c2_tip: Flush (9 outs) + open-ended straight (8 outs) = ~15 outs (discounting overlaps). By the rule of 4, about 60%: play aggressive, it's a premium semi-bluff.
math_int.c3_p1: Counting too many outs is a common mistake. A dirty out is a card that improves your hand, but that also completes a better hand for the villain. Example: you have a low straight on a same-suit board; the card that finishes your straight may be exactly the one that gives the villain the flush, so it's not a real out. When estimating equity, discount the outs that probably hand him the better hand. Better to count fewer and right than to inflate the equity and make a bad call.
math_int.c3_coach: Before multiplying the outs, ask: 'does this card give me the BEST hand, or just a hand better than my current one?'. If it also helps the villain, it's dirty: count it as half, or don't count it. A draw to the nuts is what earns the full outs.
math_int.c4_p1: The intermediate's mental leap is to stop calculating against a single hand and start thinking against the villain's range. You don't face 'the set'; you face the set of hands he'd play this way, which includes sets, but also draws, worse pairs and bluffs. Your real equity is the average against that whole set. Often the hand that scares you is just a small slice of his range, and your average equity is much better than the fear suggests. Calculate against the set, not against the nightmare.
math_int.c4_curio: That's why the solver 'calls' hands a beginner would fold: it doesn't look at the hand that beats you, it looks at the whole range and sees that, on average, it has enough equity. Thinking in ranges is what turns fear into math.
math_int.ex_p1: Situation: you have J-T of hearts on the 9-8-2 flop with two hearts, facing a bet. Count the equity: flush draw (9 outs) + open-ended straight (8 outs) = ~15 outs; by the rule of 4, about 60%. Even if the villain has a made pair, you're the favorite. With reasonable odds, this isn't just a call: it's a great spot for a raise (semi-bluff), because you have fold equity and still win big when you complete.
math_int.ex_p2: Compare with a dirty out: imagine that, instead of J-T of hearts, you had J-T of clubs on a two-heart board. Your straight still counts, but the Q or 7 of hearts that finishes your straight can give the villain the flush: some of your outs turned dirty, and your real equity drops. Common mistake: counting 15 clean outs in both cases. In the first, you have the nut flush draw alongside; in the second, part of your outs helps the enemy. Counting right is what separates the good call from the bad call.
math_int.cmp_r1_rec: Doesn't know the equities by heart; guesses in the all-in.
math_int.cmp_r2_rec: Counts every out, clean or not.
math_int.cmp_r2_pro: Discounts dirty outs and values combined draws.
math_int.cmp_r3_rec: Calculates against the hand that scares them most.
math_int.cmp_r3_reg: Thinks about a few of the villain's hands.
math_int.cmp_r3_pro: Estimates the average equity against the whole range.
math_int.e1_why: Common matchups have known equity; guessing leads to costly mistakes.
math_int.e1_fix: Memorize: coinflip ~55, pair vs pair ~80, dominated ~70.
math_int.e2_why: Outs that give the villain a better hand inflate your equity.
math_int.e2_fix: Discount the outs that also help the villain.
math_int.e3_why: Flush + open-ended straight is ~15 outs, almost a favorite.
math_int.e3_fix: Add up the outs of both draws; play aggressive.
math_int.e4_m: Calculating against the hand that scares you most.
math_int.e4_why: That hand is just a slice of the range; the average is much better.
math_int.e4_fix: Estimate the equity against the villain's whole range.
math_int.e5_m: Using the rule of 4 on the turn (only one card left).
math_int.e5_why: On the turn you see only the river; the rule is the 2.
math_int.e5_fix: Turn: outs x2. Flop with both cards to come: x4.
math_int.e6_m: Confusing favorite against a hand with favorite against a range.
math_int.e6_why: You can lose to the specific hand and still have good equity vs the range.
math_int.e6_fix: Separate 'I lose to this hand' from 'I have equity against the set'.
math_int.chk1: Is it a classic matchup? Do I know the equity by heart?
math_int.chk2: How many outs do I have, adding up combined draws?
math_int.chk3: Is any out dirty (gives the villain a better hand)? Did I discount it?
math_int.chk4: Am I calculating against the right hand, or against the range?
math_int.chk5: Am I using the right rule (x4 on the flop, x2 on the turn)?
math_int.drill_sub: Estimate equity, add up outs and compare with pot odds in intermediate spots. Each correct answer earns XP.
math_int.t1: Memorize the classic matchups: coinflip ~55, pair vs pair ~80, dominated ~70.
math_int.t2: Combined draws add outs: flush + straight ~15 outs (~60%).
math_int.t3: Discount dirty outs that give the villain a better hand.
math_int.t4: Calculate equity against the range, not against the hand that scares you most.
math_int.g1_def: A matchup near 50/50, typically a pair against two high cards.
math_int.g2_def: A hand that shares a card with a better hand (e.g., AQ against AK).
math_int.g3_def: A card that gives you the best hand without improving the villain.
math_int.g4_def: A card that improves your hand, but gives the villain an even better one.
math_int.g5_def: Having two draws at the same time (flush + straight); many outs added together.
math_int.g6_def: Your average chance of winning against the whole set of the villain's hands.

board.title: Hand strength on the board: where you stand
board.subtitle: Knowing where your hand stands on the board is the foundation of every postflop decision. Learn to classify your hand, the equity buckets, and why strength is always relative. With a table and drills.
board.intro_p1: Before you decide whether to bet, call, or fold postflop, there is a question that comes first: where does my hand stand on this board? Do you have a strong, medium, or trash hand? Are you ahead or behind? Beginners skip this question and act on impulse; solid players classify their hand on every flop, and that read guides everything that follows.
board.intro_p2: In this lesson you will learn to read the strength of your hand in an organized way: classify what you have, fit it into equity 'buckets' (from the nuts to trash), and understand why the same hand can be strong on one board and weak on another. Hand strength is not a fixed grade; it is always relative to the board and to what the villain can hold.
board.intro_note: Every postflop decision (betting for value, bluffing, calling, folding) depends on knowing where you stand. Misjudging your own hand is the root of most mistakes: betting a weak hand thinking it is strong, or folding a hand that was ahead.
board.obj1: Classify your hand on the board: top/middle/bottom pair, two pair, set, draw, or air.
board.obj2: Fit the hand into the four equity buckets (nuts, value, marginal, air).
board.obj3: Understand that strength is relative to the board and to the villain's range.
board.obj4: Know who has the advantage on the board and what realizing equity means.
board.fund_p1: Hand strength is how good your hand is RIGHT NOW, on the current board. A pair of kings is strong on a low flop, but weak on a flop with an Ace. Texture is the shape of the board: dry (disconnected cards) or wet (connected, suited). And range is the set of hands the villain can hold. Your hand strength only makes sense compared to the texture and the range: you do not play against your cards, you play against theirs.
board.fund_p2: To organize this, players split hands into equity buckets: the nuts (the best hand or close to it), value (good hands that want to bet and get called), marginal hands (medium hands that prefer to control the pot), and air (nothing, which only wins by bluffing). Knowing which bucket you are in tells you the right action: value bets, marginal controls, air folds or bluffs.
board.c1_p1: The first step, on every flop, is to name what you have. Did you make a pair? Of which card: the highest on the board (top pair), a middle one, or the lowest? Did you make two pair, a set, a straight, a flush? Or did you make nothing (air), holding a draw at best? This simple classification, done in seconds, is the foundation of the decision. In the scene, you have A-K on a A-8-3 flop: you made top pair with the best kicker, a strong value hand.
board.c1_cap: You have A-K on a A-8-3 flop. You made top pair (the Ace) with the best kicker (the King): a strong value hand, that wants to bet and get called.
board.c1_key: On every flop, name your hand before you act: top pair? middle pair? two pair? draw? air? Without this step, everything else is a guess.
board.c2_p1: After classifying, fit the hand into a bucket, because the bucket tells you the action. Nuts: the best hand or close to it (set, top flush); wants to build a big pot. Value: a good hand that beats a lot of what calls (good top pair, two pair); bets for value. Marginal: a medium hand (middle pair, top pair with a weak kicker); prefers to control the pot, often with a check. Air: nothing; only wins if the villain folds, so it folds or is used as a bluff with a draw attached. The bucket, not a hunch, decides whether you bet, control, or fold.
board.c2_tip: Value bets to get called. Marginal controls the pot. Nuts builds the big pot. Air folds, or bluffs only with a draw. First the bucket, then the action.
board.c3_p1: The same hand changes bucket depending on the board and the villain. Top pair on a dry flop (A-8-3) is strong value: little beats you. That same top pair on a wet flop (J-T-9 two-tone) becomes marginal: straights, two pair, and draws threaten you, and betting too much only gets you called when you are behind. And against a tight range (someone who only continues with strong hands), even a good hand loses value. Never read your hand alone; read it against the texture and against what the villain can hold.
board.c3_coach: Before deciding your hand is 'strong', ask: strong against what? A top pair is great against the range of someone who calls wide, and mediocre against the range of someone who only raises the best. Strength is a comparison, not a medal.
board.c4_title: Chapter 4: who has the advantage on the board
board.c4_p1: Beyond your hand, look at the board as a whole: who has the advantage here? On a high-card flop (A-K-4), whoever opened the pot has more strong hands, it is their range that 'hits' the board. On a low, connected flop (7-6-5), the advantage tends to flip to the caller. This is the equity distribution: on some boards one player has far more nutted hands than the other, and whoever has that advantage can bet more and bigger. Knowing whose board it is matters as much as knowing your own hand.
board.c4_warn: Having a decent hand on a board that is the villain's is dangerous: you can be 'ahead' of the average and still lose a lot, because the hands that call you are precisely the ones that beat you. Board advantage is worth more than an isolated hand.
board.c5_p1: Having equity is not the same as realizing equity. Realization is how much of your theoretical chance you actually turn into won pots. The same hand realizes more equity in position (you see what the villain does before you act, control the pot size, and reach showdown cheaper) and less out of position. That is why a marginal hand is worth more on the button than in the BB: the equity does not change, how much of it you can capture does. Position is what makes the same card worth more.
board.c5_curio: That is why players open more hands on the button and fewer from UTG: it is not that the cards change, it is that equity realization is much higher when you play the rest of the hand in position.
board.ex_p1: Situation: you have K-Q on a Q-7-2 rainbow flop. Classify: you made top pair (the Queen) with a good kicker (the King), on a dry board. Bucket: value. Advantage: the high-card board favors whoever opened, and you are the aggressor. Conclusion: a value hand that wants to bet and get called, with a small c-bet (dry board).
board.ex_p2: Now change the board to Q-J-T two-tone. Your hand is still top pair, but the bucket changed: now it is marginal. Straights (A-K, 9-8), two pair, and flush draws threaten, and the board advantage is more the villain's. Betting big here only gets you called when you are behind. Common mistake: treating top pair as strong value on both boards. The card is the same; the strength is not. Always classify against the board.
board.cmp_r1_rec: Looks only at their own cards ('I have a pair').
board.cmp_r1_reg: Classifies the hand, but forgets the villain's range.
board.cmp_r1_pro: Reads the hand against the board and against their range.
board.cmp_r2_rec: Treats every top pair as a strong hand.
board.cmp_r2_reg: Notices bad boards, but still bets too much.
board.cmp_r2_pro: Adjusts the bucket by texture (value on dry, marginal on wet).
board.cmp_r3_rec: Plays the hand the same from any seat.
board.cmp_r3_reg: Knows position helps, but does not adjust much.
board.cmp_r3_pro: Exploits realization: plays more and better in position.
board.e1_m: Acting without classifying your own hand.
board.e1_why: Without knowing where you stand, every decision becomes a guess.
board.e1_fix: On every flop, name the hand before you act.
board.e2_m: Treating every top pair as a strong hand.
board.e2_why: Top pair on a wet board is marginal; betting too much gets called when you are behind.
board.e2_fix: Adjust the bucket by the board texture.
board.e3_m: Reading the hand without looking at the villain's range.
board.e3_why: A good hand vs a wide range becomes mediocre vs a tight range.
board.e3_fix: Ask: strong against what? Compare it to their range.
board.e4_m: Ignoring whose board advantage it is.
board.e4_why: A decent hand on the villain's board gets you called by exactly what beats you.
board.e4_fix: Before inflating the pot, see which range 'hits' that board.
board.e5_m: Overvaluing marginal hands out of position.
board.e5_why: Out of position you realize less equity; the hand is worth less.
board.e5_fix: Out of position, be more selective and control the pot.
board.e6_m: Confusing air with a strong draw.
board.e6_why: Air with no draw does not win by calling; only by folding it out or bluffing.
board.e6_fix: If you have no pair and no draw, the bucket is air: fold cheaply.
board.chk1: What did I make? (top pair, middle pair, two pair, draw, air)
board.chk2: Which bucket does it fall into? (nuts, value, marginal, air)
board.chk3: Is the board dry or wet? Does that change my bucket?
board.chk4: Whose advantage is it on this board?
board.chk5: Am I in position? How much of that equity can I realize?
board.drill_sub: Classify your hand and the board texture, spots generated for you. Each correct answer is worth XP.
board.t1: Before you act, classify the hand on the board: that is the foundation of everything.
board.t2: Fit it into the bucket: nuts builds, value bets, marginal controls, air folds.
board.t3: Strength is relative to the board and to the villain's range, not a fixed grade.
board.t4: You realize more equity in position; the same hand is worth more on the button.
board.g1_def: How good your hand is right now, on the current board, compared to what the villain can hold.
board.g2_def: The shape of the board: dry (disconnected cards) or wet (connected or suited).
board.g3_def: Groups of hands by strength: nuts, value, marginal, and air; each calls for an action.
board.g4_def: A pair made with the highest card on the board.
board.g5_def: Having more strong hands than the villain on a given board; it lets you bet more.
board.g6_def: Turning your theoretical chance into won pots; position increases realization.
board.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads your hands and shows where reading strength is costing you chips.
board.gl1: Classifies your hand strength and the board texture on every postflop decision.
board.gl2: Flags value bets that are too weak or bluffs with a hand that should control.
board.gl3: Marks when you overvalued the hand on a board that was the villain's.
board.gl4: Measures your equity realization in position vs out of position.

showdown.title: Showdown Value: the hand that just wants to see the cards
showdown.subtitle: Not every hand wants to bet. Learn to recognize the medium hands that win at showdown, why they prefer to check, and when to use them to call an aggressor.
showdown.intro_p1: Beginners tend to think of two categories of hand: the strong ones (I bet for value) and the trash (I bluff or fold). But there is a third one, and it is the most poorly played of all: the medium hand. It is not strong enough to bet for value, nor weak enough to bluff. What it has is showdown value: it beats bluffs if the hand reaches showdown. And the right play with it is almost never to bet.
showdown.intro_p2: Knowing how to play showdown value hands is what separates those who 'bleed' chips from those who reach showdown cheaply and win the pots they deserve. In this lesson you will learn to recognize these hands, understand why betting them is a classic mistake, how to control the pot with them, and when to use them to call an aggressive player.
showdown.intro_note: Strong = bets for value. Trash = bluffs or folds. Medium (showdown value) = check to see the showdown, or call to catch bluffs. Recognizing the third category already improves your postflop a lot.
showdown.obj1: Recognize a hand with showdown value (beats bluffs, loses to value).
showdown.obj2: Understand why betting these hands is usually a mistake.
showdown.obj3: Use the check to control the pot and reach showdown cheaply.
showdown.obj4: Know when the medium hand becomes a bluff-catcher against an aggressor.
showdown.fund_p1: Showdown value (SDV) is the value your hand has if it simply reaches showdown with no more bets. A hand has SDV when it beats the hands that would bluff, but loses to the hands that would bet for value. Typical examples: a small or medium pair, a high Ace with no pair, a bottom pair on the board. It is not a hand to build a pot with; it is a hand to take to showdown.
showdown.fund_p2: The core idea is that, with SDV, betting hurts you. If you bet, the only hands that call are the better ones (which beat you), and the hands you would beat (the villain's bluffs and trash) simply fold. You trade a showdown you would win for a pot you lose. That is why the natural play with SDV is the check: you are already ahead of the weak hands, so you just need to reach showdown for free.
showdown.c1_p1: The first step is to identify the hand. Ask yourself: 'does my hand beat a bluff, but lose to a value bet?'. If the answer is yes, you have showdown value. In the scene, you have a pair of 7s on a K-9-4-2-J board. That pair beats any bluff (high cards that missed, draws that did not complete), but loses to any King, Queen, Jack, or better pair. It is the picture of showdown value: good enough to win at showdown, too weak to bet.
showdown.c1_cap: Pair of 7s on the river (board K-9-4-2-J). You beat the villain's bluffs, but lose to any better pair. It is a showdown value hand: it wants to reach showdown, not bet.
showdown.c1_key: A hand has showdown value when it BEATS bluffs but LOSES to value. If that is your case, think check, not bet.
showdown.c2_title: Chapter 2: why betting is a mistake
showdown.c2_p1: Betting a showdown value hand is the worst of both worlds, and it is worth understanding well why. When you bet, the villain folds everything you would beat (the bluffs and the weak hands) and calls only with what beats you. In other words: you make their bad hands fold and turn your edge over them into nothing, while growing the pot precisely against the hands that beat you. It is playing against yourself. The check preserves the value: you beat the bluffs at showdown, without building a pot you lose.
showdown.c2_tip: Bet SDV? Only what beats you calls; the rest folds. You lose both ends. Check is almost always better.
showdown.c3_p1: With showdown value, your goal is to reach showdown spending the least. This is called controlling the pot: keeping the pot small so you can call cheaply to the end, or simply check and see the cards for free. In position, the check-behind is the perfect tool: you close the street without putting in chips and still deny the villain the chance to raise you. Out of position, you check and decide whether to call their bet or not. The rule: with a medium hand, a small pot is your friend.
showdown.c3_coach: When you are torn between betting 'for protection' and checking with a medium hand, prefer the check. Most of the hands you want to 'protect' against were not going to call you anyway; you are only inflating the pot against the better ones.
showdown.c4_p1: The showdown value hand has a second use: calling. If the villain is aggressive and bluffs too much, your medium hand stops being passive and becomes a bluff-catcher: it loses to value, but beats all of their bluffs, and against someone who bluffs a lot, that is profit. It is the same pair of 7s: against a passive player who only bets value, you fold to a bet; against a maniac who bluffs every river, you call. The hand is the same; the villain decides whether it becomes a call or a fold.
showdown.c4_warn: Not every medium hand should call. Against a passive player, who almost never bluffs, showdown value becomes an easy fold when they bet: if they only bet value, your medium hand loses. Only become a bluff-catcher against someone who bluffs.
showdown.ex_p1: Situation: you have a pair of 7s on the river, board K-9-4-2-J. The villain checks to you (did not bet). What do you do? Check behind. Why: your hand beats all the bluffs they checked, but if you bet, they fold those bluffs and call only with Kx or better. By checking, you reach showdown for free and beat the bluffs. Betting would be playing against yourself.
showdown.ex_p2: Now change it: instead of checking, the villain shoves all-in on the river. Call or fold? It depends on them. If they are a passive player who only bets value, fold: your medium hand loses to their betting range. If they are an aggressor who bluffs too much, call: your hand becomes a bluff-catcher and beats all the bluffs. Common mistake: betting the pair of 7s outright 'to not give a free card'. This hand does not need protection; it needs a cheap showdown.
showdown.cmp_r1_rec: Bets 'for protection' or because they do not know what to do.
showdown.cmp_r1_reg: Usually checks, but sometimes bets with no value.
showdown.cmp_r2_rec: Calls out of curiosity or folds out of fear, without reading the villain.
showdown.cmp_r2_reg: Reads the villain sometimes, but hesitates.
showdown.cmp_r2_pro: Becomes a bluff-catcher vs an aggressor; folds vs a passive.
showdown.cmp_r3_rec: Inflates the pot with medium hands.
showdown.cmp_r3_pro: Keeps the pot small with showdown value.
showdown.e1_why: You make the bluffs fold and only get called by what beats you.
showdown.e1_fix: With showdown value, prefer to check and reach showdown cheaply.
showdown.e2_m: Folding medium hands against a maniac.
showdown.e2_why: Against someone who bluffs a lot, your hand becomes a profitable bluff-catcher.
showdown.e2_fix: Vs an aggressor, call with showdown value.
showdown.e3_m: Calling medium hands against a passive player.
showdown.e3_why: A passive player almost only bets value; your medium hand loses.
showdown.e3_fix: Vs a passive who bets, fold the showdown value hand.
showdown.e4_m: Not recognizing the medium hand as its own category.
showdown.e4_why: Treating everything as value or trash leads to betting what you should check.
showdown.e4_fix: Learn the third category: beats bluffs, loses to value.
showdown.e5_m: Inflating the pot with showdown value.
showdown.e5_why: A big pot forces you to call expensively precisely when you are medium.
showdown.e5_fix: Control the pot: keep it small with medium hands.
showdown.e6_m: Bluffing with a hand that already has showdown value.
showdown.e6_why: You throw away the chance to win for free at showdown.
showdown.e6_fix: If the hand already beats bluffs, do not turn it into a bluff; take it to showdown.
showdown.chk1: Does my hand beat bluffs but lose to value? (it is showdown value)
showdown.chk2: If I bet, who calls? Only what beats me?
showdown.chk3: Can I check and reach showdown cheaply?
showdown.chk4: Is the villain aggressive (bluffs) or passive (only value)?
showdown.chk5: If they bet: do I call (vs aggressor) or fold (vs passive)?
showdown.drill_sub: Medium-hand spots: check, why not to bet, and when to call. Each correct answer is worth XP.
showdown.t1: There is a third category: the medium hand, with showdown value.
showdown.t2: Showdown value beats bluffs and loses to value: usually check.
showdown.t3: Betting SDV is playing against yourself: only what beats you calls.
showdown.t4: Against an aggressor, the medium hand becomes a bluff-catcher; against a passive, fold.
showdown.g1_def: The value of your hand if it reaches showdown; beats bluffs, loses to value.
showdown.g2_def: A medium hand used to call the bets of a villain who bluffs a lot.
showdown.g3_def: Keeping the pot small to reach showdown cheaply with a medium hand.
showdown.g4_def: Checking in position, closing the street without betting; the showdown value tool.
showdown.g5_def: Neither strong enough for value, nor weak enough to bluff; the territory of showdown value.
showdown.g6_def: Someone who almost never bluffs; against them, medium hands fold when they bet.
showdown.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads your hands and shows where showdown value was played poorly.
showdown.gl1: Flags bets with medium hands that should have checked.
showdown.gl2: Marks bad folds of bluff-catchers against aggressive villains.
showdown.gl3: Signals bad calls with a medium hand against passive players.
showdown.gl4: Crosses the decision with the villain's archetype (aggressive vs passive) in the HUD.

position.title: Position Fundamentals: your most valuable asset
position.subtitle: You don't get to choose your cards, but you do choose how you use position. Learn why acting last decides so many pots and how position changes which hands you play.
position.intro_p1: If there is a single concept that separates winners from losers in poker, it isn't a magic hand or a brilliant bluff: it's position. Position is about the order in which players act. Whoever acts last has a huge edge, because they decide already knowing what everyone else did. Unlike cards, which are luck, position is a structural advantage that repeats hand after hand.
position.intro_p2: The most common beginner mistake is playing the same hands from any seat, ignoring position. In this lesson you'll understand why acting last is worth so much, why the same hand earns more in position, and how that changes how many hands you should play from each spot at the table. Position isn't a detail; it's the foundation of nearly every preflop and postflop decision.
position.intro_note: A good hand shows up now and then; position you have on a quarter of hands (on the button), guaranteed. Exploiting position well is profit that doesn't depend on luck, and that's why it's the first fundamental to master.
position.obj1: Understand what position is and why acting last is an advantage.
position.obj2: Know the difference between in position (IP) and out of position (OOP).
position.obj3: Understand why the same hand earns more in position.
position.obj4: Adjust how many hands to play based on your seat at the table.
position.fund_p1: Position is the order in which you act in a hand. Being in position (IP) means acting after the villain postflop; out of position (OOP) means acting before. At a table, positions run from the early ones (UTG, the first to speak preflop) to the late ones (CO and BTN), plus the blinds (SB and BB), who post blind and play out of position postflop. The button (BTN) is the seat that acts last on every postflop street.
position.fund_p2: The advantage of acting last is information. When it's your turn, you've already seen what the others did (bet, checked, folded), and you decide with more certainty. Out of position, you act in the dark and can still get raised by someone behind you. That information gap adds up over the hand and is the root of everything that follows in this lesson.
position.c1_p1: Acting last is like playing with an extra card up your sleeve: you see the movie before deciding. If the villain bets, you know they have something (or are bluffing) before you put chips in; if they check, you can bet or take a free card, whichever you prefer. Out of position, you don't have that luxury: you bet without knowing their reaction and can be raised. In the scene, you're on the button: the CO and the BB will act, and you decide last, with everyone's information.
position.c1_cap: You're on the button (BTN). Postflop, the CO and the BB act before you: you decide last, always with maximum information. It's the best seat at the table.
position.c1_key: In position you act last and decide already knowing what the villain did. That extra information is what makes position so valuable.
position.c2_title: Chapter 2: the same hand earns more in position
position.c2_p1: Here's the point many beginners miss: the same hand is worth more in position than out of it. Why? In position you control the pot size (bet when you want value, check back to see cards for free), reach showdown more cheaply, and bluff more effectively. Out of position you have less control and realize less of your chance to win. This is the concept of equity realization: the theoretical chance is the same, but in position you turn it into won pots more often.
position.c2_tip: Equity is the same in both seats; realization is not. In position you cash in more of your chance, so the same hand is worth more.
position.c3_title: Chapter 3: how many hands to play by position
position.c3_p1: Since position changes the value of hands, it changes how many you should play. From the early positions (UTG), with the whole table still to act, you open tight: only strong hands, because plenty of people can have something better and you'll play out of position more often. Near the button, you open much wider: few players are behind and you'll play in position. That's why opening ranges grow as you get closer to the button. Playing loose early and tight late is one of the most common leaks; the right way is the opposite.
position.c3_coach: If you remember only one rule from this lesson, make it this: open tight in early positions and wide near the button. That alone fixes a big chunk of a beginner's preflop mistakes.
position.c4_title: Chapter 4: the button and the blinds
position.c4_p1: The extremes make everything clear. The button is the best seat at the table: you act last postflop every hand, so you steal more blinds, play more hands, and make more money from there than from anywhere else. The blinds (SB and BB) are the worst: you've already put chips in blind and you'll play postflop out of position. That's why the BB defends carefully and the SB, the worst seat of all, plays even tighter. Love the button; respect the blinds.
position.c4_warn: Don't fall in love with a pretty hand in the SB or the BB: out of position, even decent hands earn less. And be careful calling from the BB just because 'it's cheap': you'll play the rest of the hand in the dark.
position.ex_p1: Situation: you're dealt K-9 suited, a medium-speculative hand. From UTG (first position), the right play is fold: with the whole table behind, this hand plays badly, often out of position and dominated. On the BTN, that same K-9s is an easy open: few players behind and you'll play in position, using the hand well.
position.ex_p2: The same hand, two opposite decisions, purely because of position. That sums up the lesson: there's no 'K-9 suited is playable or not' in a vacuum; there's 'K-9 suited from which seat?'. Common mistake: playing the hand the same from both spots, whether folding good hands on the button or calling trash from UTG. Position is the first question, before you even look closely at the cards.
position.cmp_r1_rec: Plays the same hands from any seat.
position.cmp_r1_reg: Adjusts a bit, but opens too wide early.
position.cmp_r1_pro: Tight early, wide near the button.
position.cmp_r2_reg: Knows the button is good, but doesn't pressure enough.
position.cmp_r2_pro: Steals and plays many hands from the button.
position.cmp_r3_rec: Defends the blinds wide, out of stubbornness.
position.cmp_r3_reg: Defends reasonably, but gets into trouble out of position.
position.e1_m: Playing the same hands from any position.
position.e1_why: Position changes the value of the hand; ignoring it leaks EV.
position.e1_fix: Tighten up early, open wide near the button.
position.e2_why: With the whole table behind, weak hands play badly and out of position.
position.e2_fix: From UTG, open only strong hands.
position.e3_m: Folding good hands on the button.
position.e3_why: On the button you play in position; many hands turn into profit.
position.e3_fix: Widen your opening range near the button.
position.e4_why: You'll play postflop out of position, realizing less equity.
position.e4_fix: Defend the blinds selectively, especially the SB.
position.e5_m: Ignoring who's behind you when opening.
position.e5_why: The more people behind, the greater the chance someone has a better hand.
position.e5_fix: Count the positions still to act before opening.
position.e6_why: The advantage of acting last applies on every postflop street.
position.e6_fix: Remember position on every decision, from the flop to the river.
position.chk1: Which position am I in? (early, late, blind)
position.chk2: How many players still act after me?
position.chk3: Will I play this hand in position or out of it?
position.chk4: Is this hand strong enough for my seat?
position.chk5: Am I exploiting the button and respecting the blinds?

position.t1: Position is the order of acting; in position you decide last, with more information.
position.t2: The same hand earns more in position (realizes more equity).
position.t3: Open tight early (UTG) and wide near the button.
position.g1_def: The order in which players act in a hand.
position.g2_def: Acting after the villain postflop; the advantageous position.
position.g3_def: Acting before the villain postflop; the disadvantageous position.
position.g4_def: The seat that acts last postflop; the best at the table.
position.g5_def: The forced bets and the seats that play postflop out of position.
position.g6_def: How much of your theoretical chance you turn into won pots; higher in position.
