ranges.intro_p1: The first decision of every hand is: do I enter the pot or not? When no one has opened before you, entering means putting in a raise (what we call RFI, raise-first-in). Which hands are worth that raise? The answer isn't a loose list of good hands; it's a range: the exact set of hands you open from each position. Playing correct opening ranges is the preflop fundamental that most impacts your results.
ranges.intro_p2: In this lesson you'll understand why we think in ranges (and not in isolated hands), why the range changes with position, and how to read the range chart (the 13x13 grid). Right below, you practice in the quiz: pick the GTO opening action in real spots and see the correct range. It's learning and practicing in the same place.
ranges.intro_note: RFI (raise-first-in) is opening the pot with a raise when everyone before you has folded. It's the most common preflop situation and the basis of your entire opening strategy.
ranges.obj1: Understand why we play in ranges, not isolated hands.
ranges.obj2: Know that the opening range changes with position.
ranges.obj4: Recognize, by position, roughly how much to open.
ranges.fund_p1: Range is the set of hands you play a certain way in a situation. Your UTG opening range, for example, is the exact group of hands you'd raise from there. Thinking in ranges, instead of 'is this hand good?', is what gives your game consistency: you don't decide on impulse, you decide by the rule that applies to every hand in that group.
ranges.fund_p2: Ranges are represented on a 13x13 grid. The pairs sit on the diagonal (AA, KK, ... 22); above the diagonal are the suited hands (same suit, e.g. AKs); below are the offsuit ones (e.g. AKo). Each cell is colored by the action (raise, call, fold) and by frequency. Reading this grid is like reading a map: at a glance you see what to open and what to muck.
ranges.c1_title: Chapter 1: play in ranges, not in hands
ranges.c1_p1: The beginner asks 'is this hand playable?'. The solid player asks 'is this hand in my range from here?'. The difference is huge: thinking in ranges gives you a consistent rule, keeps you from playing the same hand different ways by mood, and keeps you balanced (with value and bluffs in the right proportion). Your opening range is a decision made calmly, away from the table; in the hand, you just execute it.
ranges.c1_key: Don't ask 'is this hand good?'. Ask 'is it in my opening range for this position?'. The range is the rule; the hand is just one case of it.
ranges.c2_title: Chapter 2: the range changes with position
ranges.c2_p1: As you saw in the Position lesson, the closer to the button, the more hands are worth playing. That translates directly to opening ranges: from UTG (first position), you open tight, only the strongest hands; from the button, you open much wider. There's no single opening range; there's one for each seat. Opening the same number of hands from any position is one of the most common and most expensive preflop mistakes.
ranges.c2_tip: Tighten up in early positions, open wide near the button. The range grows as you move toward the BTN.
ranges.c3_p1: Tap the Range chart button (over here, in the quiz) and you see the grid for the situation. How to read it: the diagonal is the pairs; the upper triangle is suited; the lower one is offsuit. The colors show the action, and a striped cell means a mixed play (sometimes raise, sometimes fold). You don't need to memorize the whole grid; you need to understand the pattern: strong hands concentrated in the top corner, trash in the bottom corner, and the border shifting with position.
ranges.c3_coach: When in doubt about a border hand, check the chart and look not only at it but at its neighbors: understanding why A5s opens and A4o doesn't is what teaches you the range, not memorizing cell by cell.
ranges.c4_title: Chapter 4: how much to open from each position
ranges.c4_p1: To get a sense of scale, the table below shows, approximately, how many hands to open from each position (in tournament stacks). These aren't numbers to memorize to the letter; they're the order of magnitude. Notice how the percentage nearly triples from UTG to the button. The SB is a special case: it opens quite a lot, but plays out of position, so it has its own yardstick.
ranges.ex_p1: Situation: you're dealt A-5 suited (A5s). From UTG, this hand is outside the tight range: fold. On the button, A5s is well inside the wide range: an easy open. The same hand, two actions, because of position, exactly as the ranges dictate.
ranges.ex_p2: Why A5s and not A5o? Because suited is worth quite a bit more: the flush draw adds equity and playability, so A5s enters the range from more positions than A5o. Common mistake: treating A5s and A5o as the same hand. On the grid, they sit in different cells precisely because they play differently. Practice in the quiz below and you'll internalize these borders.
ranges.cmp_r1_rec: Opens 'hands that look good', with no range.
ranges.cmp_r1_reg: Has ranges, but applies them the same from every position.
ranges.cmp_r1_pro: Position-specific range, tight early and wide late.
ranges.cmp_r2_rec: Treats A5s and A5o as the same.
ranges.cmp_r2_reg: Knows suited is better, but overrates how much.
ranges.cmp_r2_pro: Opens suited from more positions; separates the two well.
ranges.cmp_r3_pro: Understands the grid's pattern, not just the cells.
ranges.e1_m: Opening the same number of hands from any position.
ranges.e1_why: The correct range changes a lot by position; a single one leaks EV.
ranges.e1_fix: Tighten up at UTG, open wide on the button.
ranges.e2_m: Thinking in isolated hands, not in ranges.
ranges.e2_why: Without a range, you play the same hand different ways.
ranges.e2_fix: Ask whether the hand is in the position's range, not whether it's 'good'.
ranges.e3_m: Treating suited and offsuit as the same.
ranges.e3_why: Suited has more equity and playability; it comes in from more positions.
ranges.e3_fix: Open more suited hands; separate A5s from A5o.
ranges.e4_why: With the whole table behind, weak hands play badly and out of position.
ranges.e4_fix: From UTG, only the strongest hands.
ranges.e5_m: Memorizing the grid cell by cell.
ranges.e5_why: Without understanding the pattern, you freeze on border hands.
ranges.e5_fix: Understand the pattern (strong at the top, border shifting by position).
ranges.e6_m: Ignoring the range chart during practice.
ranges.e6_why: The chart is the answer key that teaches the borders.
ranges.e6_fix: Consult the chart on doubtful hands and observe the neighbors.
ranges.chk1: Has no one opened before? (it's an RFI spot)
ranges.chk3: Is this hand in the opening range for this position?
ranges.chk4: Is it suited or offsuit? Does that change the decision?
ranges.chk5: When in doubt, did I check the chart and the neighboring cells?
ranges.t1: Play in ranges, not in hands: the range is the position's rule.
ranges.t2: The opening range grows from UTG to the button.
ranges.t3: Suited opens from more positions than offsuit.
ranges.t4: Read the grid by the pattern; the chart is your answer key.
ranges.g1_def: Raise-first-in: opening the pot with a raise when everyone before has folded.
ranges.g2_def: The set of hands you play a certain way in a situation.
ranges.g3_def: The hand chart: pairs on the diagonal, suited above, offsuit below.
ranges.g4_def: A hand with both cards of the same suit (e.g. AKs).
ranges.g5_def: A hand with cards of different suits (e.g. AKo).
ranges.g6_def: A cell that is sometimes raise and sometimes fold (frequency).
ranges.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab compares your opens with the correct ranges by position.
ranges.gl1: Shows your GTO alignment by position on the open.
ranges.gl2: Flags opens that are too wide in early positions.
ranges.gl3: Flags hands that should open and you folded (near the button).
ranges.gl4: Compares your opening range with that of winning players at the same level.
ranges.gl5: Generates range drills from your own opening spots.
ranges.practice_note: Scroll down and pick the GTO opening action in the quiz spots. Use the 'Range chart' button whenever you're in doubt.

combos.title: Combinatorics: counting combos and blockers
combos.subtitle: Ranges are made of combinations, not abstract hands. Learn how many combos each hand has, how known cards reduce them, and how to use this to read the villain's range.
combos.intro_p1: When you think 'the villain can have AK or a pair', you are thinking in abstract hands. But not every hand is equally likely: there are 16 ways to have an unpaired hand like AK and only 6 ways to have a pair. Counting these ways, the combos, is what turns a guess ('he could have a set') into a read ('he has 3 combos of a set and 12 of a draw, so most of the time he does not have the set').
combos.intro_p2: Combinatorics looks like hard math, but it is just two numbers to memorize and one simple idea: cards you already see (yours and the ones on the board) reduce the possible combos. In this lesson you will learn to count combos, understand how a blocker cuts the villain's hands, and use this to know whether he has more value or more bluffs.
combos.intro_note: Counting combos is the difference between 'I think he is bluffing here' and 'he has 4 bluff combos and 9 value combos, so calling is bad'. It is the tool that makes range reading concrete, and it is the foundation for the next lesson, on blockers.
combos.obj1: Know how many combos each type of hand has (pair, suited, offsuit).
combos.obj2: Understand how known cards reduce the possible combos.
combos.obj3: Use blockers: how holding a card cuts the villain's hands.
combos.obj4: Compare value vs bluff combos to decide call or fold.
combos.fund_p1: A combo is a specific combination of two cards. AK is not a single hand: it is the A of hearts with the K of spades, the A of diamonds with the K of clubs, and so on. The base numbers: a pair has 6 combos (you pick 2 of the 4 cards of that rank); an unpaired hand has 16, being 4 suited (one per suit) and 12 offsuit. Memorize these three numbers: 6, 4 and 12.
combos.fund_p2: The second idea is that known cards reduce combos. If you have an Ace in your hand, only 3 Aces are left in the deck, so the villain can no longer have all 6 combos of AA: only 3 remain. If the board has a King, the villain's KK combos drop from 6 to 3. Every visible card cuts possibilities, and that is what players use to narrow the villain's range.
combos.c1_p1: Start with the three base numbers. The table below shows how many combos each type of hand has, with no card known. Keep these values: they are the foundation of all counting. A useful detail: since offsuit (12) is far more than the rest, unpaired hands are much more frequent than pairs, which is why sets are rare and a pair on the board changes the read so much.
combos.c1_key: Pair = 6. Suited = 4. Offsuit = 12 (unpaired total = 16). Memorize these three and you can count any range.
combos.c2_p1: Now the part that changes the game. Every card you already see (your two and the ones on the board) removes possibilities from the villain's range. Example: normally AA has 6 combos; but if there is an Ace on the board, 3 Aces are left, and AA drops to 3 combos (C(3,2) = 3). If two Aces are visible, 1 combo is left; with three, none. Whenever you count the villain's range, first remove the cards you already see: the real number is always smaller than the 'table' one.
combos.c2_tip: One known card of a pair: combos drop from 6 to 3. Two: they drop to 1. Every visible card narrows the villain's range.
combos.c3_p1: When you hold a card that is part of the villain's strong hands, you have a blocker: you block those combinations. A classic example: on the river, you have the Ace of hearts on a board with a hearts flush draw. That Ace blocks the villain's nut flush (the A of hearts would be the nut flush), so he has fewer combos of dream hands, which makes your bluff work more often. Blockers are the reason two equally weak hands are not equal for bluffing: the one that blocks the villain's value is the better bluff.
combos.c3_coach: When choosing a bluff, prefer the hand that holds the cards of the villain's strong hands. Having the Ace of the flush suit, or a card of the straight he would call with, cuts his value combos and makes him fold more. That is the topic of the next lesson.
combos.c4_title: Chapter 4: value vs bluff by the combos
combos.c4_p1: The most practical use of combinatorics is to decide call or fold by counting the villain's value and bluff combos. If on a line he can have, say, 9 value combos and only 3 bluff combos, he rarely bluffs: in favor of the fold. If he has 6 value and 8 bluff combos, he is more of a bluffer: in favor of the call. You do not even need pinpoint precision; just to know which way the scale tips. Counting combos turns 'does he have it?' into 'how many combos of each, after all?'.
combos.c4_curio: This is why a pair on the board makes players nervous: it reduces the combos of high hands and increases the relative weight of sets and full houses in the range of whoever continues. The board texture affects the count, not just the strength.
combos.ex_p1: Situation: board A-K-4-2-9, no flush possible. The villain bets big on the river. You think he has a set of Aces (AA) or is bluffing. Count: normally AA has 6 combos, but there is an Ace on the board, so only 3 combos of AA are left. Add KK (also 3, because of the King on the board): that is 6 combos of a set in total.
combos.ex_p2: Now the bluffs: how many hands with no showdown value would he bet like this? If, from the line, you estimate about 3 or 4 bluff combos, the scale tips toward value (6 sets against 3-4 bluffs), and the fold is better. Common mistake: thinking 'he COULD have a set' and calling out of fear, without counting. The set exists, but in few combos; the right decision comes from the count, not from fear. And note: if YOU held an Ace, his AA combos would drop from 3 to 1, and calling would look much better.
combos.cmp_r1_rec: Thinks in loose hands ('he could have a set').
combos.cmp_r1_reg: Knows the base numbers, but forgets to remove the seen cards.
combos.cmp_r1_pro: Counts real combos, already discounting the board and his own cards.
combos.cmp_r2_reg: Prefers to bluff with a draw, but ignores blockers.
combos.cmp_r2_pro: Bluffs with the hand that blocks the villain's value.
combos.cmp_r3_rec: Calls out of curiosity or fear.
combos.cmp_r3_pro: Compares value and bluff combos and decides by the scale.
combos.e1_m: Treating every hand as equally likely.
combos.e1_why: A pair has 6 combos, unpaired has 16; ignoring this distorts the read.
combos.e1_fix: Use the base numbers: pair 6, suited 4, offsuit 12.
combos.e2_m: Forgetting to remove the known cards.
combos.e2_why: The board and your cards reduce the villain's combos.
combos.e2_fix: Before counting, remove the visible cards: AA with an Ace on the board = 3, not 6.
combos.e3_m: Choosing a bluff without looking at blockers.
combos.e3_why: Two weak hands are not equal: the one that blocks value bluffs better.
combos.e3_fix: Prefer the bluff that holds the cards of the villain's strong hands.
combos.e4_m: Calling 'because he COULD be bluffing', without counting.
combos.e4_why: He can, but in few combos; the real chance comes from the count.
combos.e4_fix: Compare value and bluff combos before calling.
combos.e5_why: Sets depend on a pair on the board or a pocket pair: few combos.
combos.e5_fix: Remember a set is rare; do not pay off the whole pot out of fear of it.
combos.e6_m: Thinking you need an exact count at the table.
combos.e6_why: The decision comes from the direction of the scale, not the perfect number.
combos.e6_fix: Estimate value vs bluff roughly and follow the heavier side.
combos.chk1: What hands can the villain have on this line (value and bluff)?
combos.chk2: How many combos does each have? (pair 6, suited 4, offsuit 12)
combos.chk3: What cards are already visible? Did I remove them from the count?
combos.chk4: Do I hold any blocker of his strong hands?
combos.chk5: Does the scale tip toward value (fold) or bluff (call)?
combos.drill_sub: Count combos of pairs, unpaired hands and blockers. Each correct answer is worth XP.
combos.t1: Ranges are made of combos: pair = 6, suited = 4, offsuit = 12.
combos.t2: Known cards reduce combos: AA with a visible Ace drops from 6 to 3.
combos.t3: Blockers: holding the card of the villain's value makes your bluff work more.
combos.t4: Decide call or fold by comparing value vs bluff combos.
combos.g1_def: A specific combination of two cards (e.g. A of hearts with K of spades).
combos.g2_def: A card in your hand that reduces the combos of a villain's hand.
combos.g3_def: A hand with both cards of the same suit (4 combos).
combos.g4_def: A hand with cards of different suits (12 combos).
combos.g5_def: The set of all hands, measured in combos, that a player can have.
combos.g6_def: The comparison between the value and bluff combos in the villain's range.
combos.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab counts the combos for you and shows where range reading is costing you chips.
combos.gl1: Estimates the villain's range in combos, already discounting the board and your cards.
combos.gl2: Shows the ratio of value vs bluff on call or fold spots.
combos.gl3: Flags bad calls where the villain had few bluff combos.
combos.gl4: Signals your bluffs without a blocker when a better one was available.
combos.gl5: Generates combinatorics exercises from your own spots.
combos.next_sub: Combine the combo count with the defense and bluff frequencies.

blockers.title: Blockers & Unblockers: choosing good bluffs
blockers.subtitle: Not every bluff is the same. The same weak hand bluffs better or worse depending on the cards it blocks. Learn to choose the right bluff and the right call by the blockers.
blockers.intro_p1: Two equally weak hands are not equally good for bluffing. One can make the villain fold much more than the other, even with no chance to improve. The difference is in the blockers: the cards you hold that are part of the villain's hands. Holding a card of his strong hands means he has fewer of those hands, and your bluff works more.
blockers.intro_p2: This lesson is the practical application of the previous one (Combinatorics). You already know how to count combos; now you will use that to choose which hand to bluff with and which hand to call with. The golden rule has two sides: a good bluff blocks the villain's value and does not block (unblock) his folds. Let us break down both.
blockers.intro_note: Blockers explain why the solver bluffs with a specific weak hand and gives up with another equally weak one. It is the reason behind plays that look like magic, but are just combo counting applied to hand selection.
blockers.obj1: Understand what a blocker is and how it changes your bluff.
blockers.obj2: Choose, between two bluffs, the one that blocks the villain's value.
blockers.obj3: Understand unblockers: not blocking the hands the villain would fold.
blockers.fund_p1: A blocker is a card in your hand that reduces the combos of a villain's hand. If you have the Ace of hearts on a hearts flush board, the villain cannot have the nut flush with the Ace of hearts: you block his dream hand. An unblocker is the useful opposite: a card you do NOT have, leaving the villain free to have the hands you want him to have, usually the ones he will fold.
blockers.fund_p2: The golden rule of bluffing has two sides. You want to block the hands that call you (the villain's value), so he has fewer of them. And you want to not block the hands he would fold, so he has many of them to let go. The best bluff does both at once: it holds the villain's value and leaves his folds free.
blockers.c1_title: Chapter 1: bluffing by blocking the value
blockers.c1_p1: Start with the more intuitive side. On the river, a board with three hearts allows the flush. You have a weak hand, but with the Ace of hearts. That Ace blocks the nut flush: the villain simply cannot have the best flush, because the card that would make that hand is in your hand. Result: he has fewer combos of hands that call you, and your all-in bluff makes him fold much more. The same bet with a weak hand WITHOUT the Ace of hearts works far less.
blockers.c1_cap: River with three hearts (flush possible). You have a weak hand, but hold the Ace of hearts: it blocks the villain's nut flush. It is the ideal bluff here.
blockers.c1_key: Bluff with the hand that blocks the hands that would call you. Having the Ace of the flush, or the card of the straight, cuts the villain's value combos and makes him fold more.
blockers.c2_title: Chapter 2: the other side, unblock the folds
blockers.c2_p1: Blocking the value is half the story. The other half is not blocking the folds. Imagine the villain would fold middle pairs. If your bluffing hand contains the cards of those middle pairs, you reduce how many of them he can have, and fewer hands are left for him to let go, so your bluff works less. The ideal is to bluff with a hand that does NOT touch the hands he would fold, keeping his fold range as full as possible. Block the value and unblock the folds: the two together make the perfect bluff.
blockers.c2_tip: Good bluff = blocks the value + does not block the folds. If your hand 'touches' the hands he would let go, it is a worse bluff.
blockers.c3_p1: Blockers are not only for bluffing; they serve for calling too. When you have a medium hand on the river and need to decide whether to call an all-in, prefer the hand that blocks the villain's value. If you hold a card of his strong hands, he has fewer value combos, so it is more likely he is bluffing, and your call gets better. It is the same reasoning as the bluff, flipped inside out: blocking the value helps you both bluff and call.
blockers.c3_coach: When choosing between calling with two similar medium hands, call with the one that holds the villain's value (for example, an Ace when he would represent a set of Aces). Blocking his value shifts the range toward bluffs, and a bluff-catcher with a blocker is worth much more.
blockers.c4_p1: In practice, you almost always have more than one weak hand as a bluff candidate, and the right play is to bluff with one and give up with the other, not to bluff with both. Choose the one with the best blocker: the one that blocks the villain's value more and his folds less. Bluffing with the wrong hand (the one that blocks nothing, or worse, that blocks the folds) is throwing chips away, even if both hands look equally like 'trash'. The blocker is the tiebreaker.
blockers.c4_warn: Do not bluff with EVERY weak hand. Blockers exist precisely so you can choose: bluff the one that blocks the value, give up with the rest. Bluffing everything is overbluffing; bluffing nothing is passive. The blocker is the criterion.
blockers.ex_p1: Situation: river, board Q-8-3-2-9 with three hearts (hearts flush possible). You reached here with two bluff candidates: Ace of hearts with 5 (Ace of hearts, no pair) or 7-6 offsuit (nothing, no heart). Which do you bluff with?
blockers.ex_p2: Bluff with the Ace of hearts. Why: it blocks the villain's nut flush, the hand most likely to call you, so the villain has fewer value combos and folds much more. The 7-6 blocks nothing relevant: it is a weak bluff. Common mistake: bluffing with both, or bluffing with the 7-6 'because it has no showdown value'. Both are trash, but only one is a good bluff. Bluff the one with the blocker, give up with the other. That is the secret to choosing good bluffs.
blockers.cmp_r1_rec: Bluffs with any trash, or never bluffs.
blockers.cmp_r1_reg: Prefers to bluff with a draw, but ignores blockers.
blockers.cmp_r1_pro: Bluffs the hand that blocks the value and unblocks the folds.
blockers.cmp_r2_rec: Calls out of fear, without looking at the cards he holds.
blockers.cmp_r2_reg: Weighs value vs bluff, but forgets the blockers.
blockers.cmp_r2_pro: Calls with the blocker of the villain's value.
blockers.cmp_r3_reg: Feels one is 'better', without knowing why.
blockers.cmp_r3_pro: Uses the blocker as tiebreaker: bluffs one, drops the other.
blockers.e1_why: With no criterion, you bluff too much and become a call target.
blockers.e1_fix: Bluff only the one that blocks the villain's value; drop the rest.
blockers.e2_m: Ignoring blockers when choosing the bluff.
blockers.e2_why: Two weak hands are not equal: one makes the villain fold much more.
blockers.e2_fix: Prefer the hand that holds the cards of his strong hands.
blockers.e3_m: Bluffing with the hand that blocks the villain's folds.
blockers.e3_why: You reduce the hands he would let go, and the bluff works less.
blockers.e3_fix: Unblock the folds: bluff with a hand that does not touch his fold range.
blockers.e4_m: Calling without looking at what you block.
blockers.e4_why: The best bluff-catcher is the one that blocks the villain's value.
blockers.e4_fix: Between two similar calls, call with the blocker of the value.
blockers.e5_m: Confusing a blocker with a strong hand.
blockers.e5_why: Having the Ace of hearts is not having the flush; it is blocking the villain's flush.
blockers.e5_fix: A blocker is about what the VILLAIN cannot have, not about your strength.
blockers.e6_m: Bluffing with a completed draw that blocks your own calls.
blockers.e6_why: If you have the hand the villain would call, bluffing takes value from your own call.
blockers.e6_fix: Hands that block the value but have no showdown value are the best bluffs.
blockers.chk_title: Checklist when choosing a bluff (or call)
blockers.chk1: What are the villain's strong hands on this line?
blockers.chk2: Does my hand hold any of the cards of those strong hands? (blocks the value)
blockers.chk3: Does my hand touch the hands he would fold? (avoid: unblock the folds)
blockers.chk4: Among my bluff candidates, which has the best blocker?
blockers.chk5: If I am calling: do I block his VALUE (good) or his bluffs (bad)?
blockers.drill_sub: Choose the best bluff and the best call by the blockers. Each correct answer is worth XP.
blockers.t1: Two weak hands do not bluff the same: the blocker is the difference.
blockers.t2: A good bluff blocks the villain's value (he has fewer hands that call).
blockers.t3: A good bluff also does not block the folds (unblock: he has more to let go).
blockers.t4: To call, prefer the value blocker: it increases the chance it is a bluff.
blockers.g1_def: A card in your hand that reduces the combos of a villain's hand.
blockers.g2_def: Not having the cards of certain villain hands, leaving him free to have them.
blockers.g3_def: The best flush; the Ace of the flush suit is the most valuable blocker against it.
blockers.g4_def: Calling a bet with a medium hand, betting that the villain is bluffing.
blockers.g5_def: Holding cards of the villain's strong hands, reducing his value combos.
blockers.g6_def: Not holding the cards of the hands the villain would fold, keeping his fold range full.
blockers.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab looks at the cards you hold and shows when the blocker helped or was missing.
blockers.gl1: Flags bluffs without a blocker when there was a better hand to bluff.
blockers.gl2: Signals bluffs that block the villain's folds (a bad choice).
blockers.gl3: Marks good and bad bluff-catches by the presence of the value blocker.
blockers.gl4: Crosses your blockers with the villain's likely range on each river.
blockers.gl5: Generates blocker exercises from your own river spots.
blockers.next_sub: Review the combo counting that lies behind every blocker.

mdf.title: MDF & Alpha: how much to defend and how much to bluff
mdf.subtitle: Two simple calculations that tell you how often you should continue against a bet and how often your bluff needs to work. The foundation of balanced defense and aggression.
mdf.intro_p1: When someone bets, there is a 'right' frequency for continuing: defending too little lets the villain bluff for free; defending too much makes you pay off value bets with trash. On the other side, when you bluff, there is a 'right' frequency for bluffing so that the villain cannot simply call every time. These two frequencies have a name and a formula: MDF (minimum defense frequency) and alpha. They are perhaps the most useful and least understood math in poker.
mdf.intro_p2: The good news: both calculations are simple divisions that depend only on the size of the bet relative to the pot. In this lesson you will learn both formulas, what they mean, how to use them at the table and, just as important, when NOT to use them (against players who do not bluff or do not fold, the guideline changes).
mdf.intro_note: MDF and alpha come from the idea of indifference: you defend (or bluff) enough so that the villain's opposite play (bluffing more, or calling more) does not profit for free. It is the foundation of balanced play, the starting point before you exploit.
mdf.obj1: Calculate the MDF: how often to continue against a bet.
mdf.obj2: Calculate the alpha: how often your bluff needs to work.
mdf.obj3: Understand why smaller bets change both frequencies.
mdf.obj4: Know when to ignore MDF and alpha (against profiles that do not bluff or do not fold).
mdf.fund_p1: MDF (minimum defense frequency) is the minimum fraction of your range that you need to continue with (call or raise) so that the villain's bluff does not profit automatically. The formula is MDF = pot / (pot + bet). Alpha is the other side: the fraction of folds your bluff needs to generate to break even, given by alpha = bet / (pot + bet). The two add up to 1: what the villain does not fold (MDF), you cannot bluff for free (alpha).
mdf.fund_p2: The key point is that everything depends on the size of the bet relative to the pot. A large bet relative to the pot calls for less defense (you can fold more) and bluffs more cheaply (it needs fewer folds). A small bet calls for a lot of defense (you fold little) and bluffs at a higher cost. Do not memorize loose numbers: understand that a bigger bet means you defend less, and a smaller bet means you defend more.
mdf.c1_title: Chapter 1: MDF, how much to defend
mdf.c1_p1: Imagine the pot at 100 and the villain betting 100 (pot-sized). By the formula, MDF = 100 / (100 + 100) = 50%: you need to continue with half of your range so that his bluff does not profit for free. If the villain bet only 50 (half pot), MDF = 100 / 150 = 67%: now you have to defend more, because the smaller bet gives better odds and folding too easily would be exploited. Interpretation: the smaller the bet, the more of your range you are forced to keep.
mdf.c1_key: MDF = pot / (pot + bet). It is the MINIMUM fraction of your range you continue with so as not to be exploited by bluffs. Smaller bet, you defend more.
mdf.c2_title: Chapter 2: alpha, how much to bluff
mdf.c2_p1: Now your side. When you bluff, the villain can call or fold. Alpha tells you the minimum fold your bluff needs to break even: alpha = bet / (pot + bet). Bluffing pot-sized (bet 100, pot 100), alpha = 100 / 200 = 50%: the villain needs to fold half the time. Bluffing half pot, alpha = 50 / 150 = 33%: a smaller bluff needs to work less often, in other words, bluffing small is cheaper. That is why small bluffs are so common: they require fewer folds to profit.
mdf.c2_tip: Alpha = bet / (pot + bet). A pot-sized bluff needs ~50% folds; half pot, ~33%. The smaller the bet, the fewer folds the bluff needs.
mdf.c3_p1: You do not need to do the math at the table every time: memorize the most common numbers. The table below combines, for the most used bet sizes, how much you should defend (MDF) and how many folds your bluff needs (alpha). Notice the pattern: both columns move in opposite directions as the bet grows.
mdf.c4_title: Chapter 4: when to ignore MDF and alpha
mdf.c4_p1: MDF and alpha describe BALANCED play, the kind where the villain punishes you if you deviate. Against most real players, the right move is to DEVIATE. Against a calling station (who never folds), alpha does not matter: bluffing is throwing money away, so you bluff almost zero and only bet for value. Against a nit who over-folds, you can bluff much more than alpha dictates, because he gives you the fold for free. And in an ICM zone, defense drops below MDF, because busting costs you the tournament. Use MDF and alpha as the baseline; then adjust for the villain in front of you.
mdf.c4_warn: MDF and alpha are the starting point, not the final answer. Applying them blindly against someone who does not bluff or does not fold is a mistake. Balance first, then exploit.
mdf.ex_p1: Situation: pot of 60, the villain bets 60 (pot-sized) on the river. How much should you defend? MDF = 60 / (60 + 60) = 50%. So you continue with about half of your range, your best hands, and fold the rest. If you fold much more than that, an attentive villain will bluff every river and profit for free.
mdf.ex_p2: Now your side: you want to bluff that same river betting the pot. By alpha, your bluff needs to make the villain fold at least 50% to break even. Adjust for the profile: if the villain is a calling station, forget alpha, he will not fold 50%, so bluffing here loses chips; bet for value only. If he is a scared nit, he folds much more than 50%, so the bluff profits easily. The formula gives you the baseline; the profile tells you which way to deviate.
mdf.cmp_r1_rec: Calls or folds on feel, with no sense of frequency.
mdf.cmp_r1_reg: Knows MDF, but applies it the same against everyone.
mdf.cmp_r1_pro: Starts from MDF and deviates based on the villain's profile.
mdf.cmp_r2_rec: Bluffs too much or too little, with no criteria.
mdf.cmp_r2_reg: Bluffs close to alpha, but ignores the profile.
mdf.cmp_r2_pro: Uses alpha as the base and adjusts: zero vs station, more vs nit.
mdf.cmp_r3_rec: Does not connect bet size to frequency.
mdf.cmp_r3_reg: Understands that a bigger bet changes the math.
mdf.cmp_r3_pro: Chooses the size thinking about the MDF and alpha it creates.
mdf.e1_m: Defending too little against small bets.
mdf.e1_why: A small bet demands A LOT of defense; folding easily is exploited by bluffs.
mdf.e1_fix: Smaller bet, defend more: follow the MDF (half pot calls for ~67%).
mdf.e2_m: Bluffing against a calling station 'because alpha allows it'.
mdf.e2_why: He does not fold; alpha assumes balance, and he is unbalanced.
mdf.e2_fix: Against a station, bluff almost zero and bet for value only.
mdf.e3_m: Applying MDF blindly on the bubble.
mdf.e3_why: Under ICM, busting costs the tournament; defense drops below MDF.
mdf.e3_fix: Near the money, defend less than MDF (see the ICM lesson).
mdf.e4_why: MDF includes call AND raise, and it is a minimum with the best part of your range.
mdf.e4_fix: Defend with your best hands up to the MDF fraction, not with trash.
mdf.e5_m: Not connecting bet size to frequency.
mdf.e5_why: Each size creates a different MDF and alpha.
mdf.e5_fix: Before betting, think about the fold your size needs (alpha).
mdf.e6_m: Treating the baseline as the final answer.
mdf.e6_why: MDF and alpha describe balance; real profit comes from exploiting deviations.
mdf.e6_fix: Start at balance and adjust to the villain's profile.
mdf.chk_title: Checklist when facing (or making) a bet
mdf.chk1: What is the size of the bet relative to the pot?
mdf.chk2: MDF = pot / (pot + bet): how much do I need to defend with?
mdf.chk3: Alpha = bet / (pot + bet): how many folds does my bluff need?
mdf.chk4: What kind of villain is he? Does he bluff and fold close to balance?
mdf.chk5: Should I deviate from the base? (station = less bluffing; nit = more; ICM = less defense)
mdf.drill_sub: Calculate the MDF and alpha for various bet sizes. Every correct answer is worth XP.
mdf.t1: MDF = pot / (pot + bet): the minimum fraction you defend.
mdf.t2: Alpha = bet / (pot + bet): the minimum fold your bluff needs.
mdf.t3: A smaller bet = you defend more and bluff more cheaply; bigger = the opposite.
mdf.t4: MDF and alpha are the baseline; against real profiles, deviate (exploit).
mdf.g1_def: Minimum defense frequency: the minimum fraction of your range that you continue with against a bet.
mdf.g2_def: The fraction of folds your bluff needs to generate to break even.
mdf.g3_def: The point at which the villain's opposite play neither profits nor loses; the foundation of balance.
mdf.g4_def: The hands you continue with (call or raise) against a bet.
mdf.g5_def: Folding more than the MDF; exploitable by someone who bluffs a lot.
mdf.g6_def: Deviating from balance to profit from the villain's specific mistake.
mdf.gl_intro: The theory above turns into practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads your hands and shows where your defense and your bluffs drift away from balance.
mdf.gl1: Measures your fold frequency against bets and compares it with the MDF.
mdf.gl2: Flags overfolds (you gave up more than the MDF, exploitable).
mdf.gl3: Flags bad bluffs against profiles that do not fold (alpha ignored).
mdf.gl4: Cross-references your defense with the villain's archetype (station, nit) in the HUD.
mdf.gl5: Generates MDF and alpha drills from your own spots.
mdf.next_sub: Apply MDF and alpha to c-bet and barrel lines.

bet_sizing.title: Bet Sizing 101: how much to bet and why
bet_sizing.subtitle: Bet size communicates and charges a price. Learn the standard sizes for open, 3-bet and postflop, what SPR is and how to plan the three streets. With a table, a felt and a drill.
bet_sizing.intro_p1: Every bet has a size, and the size is no detail: it communicates strength and charges the opponent a price. Betting big says one thing; betting small says another. The most common beginner mistake is using a single size for everything, usually too big, without realizing they are giving away information and risking chips for nothing.
bet_sizing.intro_p2: In this lesson you will learn the standard sizes for the most common situations: the preflop open, the 3-bet and the c-bet by texture. And you will understand two concepts that tie it all together: SPR (how much is left behind relative to the pot) and pot geometry (how to plan the bets across the three streets). By the end, you will choose sizes with reasoning, not by guessing.
bet_sizing.intro_note: A bet has two functions: to extract value (charge worse hands that call) and to deny equity (make draws pay dearly or give up). The right size is the one that fulfills the function of that spot; the wrong one leaks chips or gives away the hand.
bet_sizing.obj1: Know the standard sizes for open and 3-bet (in position and out of position).
bet_sizing.obj2: Choose the c-bet size according to the board texture.
bet_sizing.obj3: Understand SPR and what it says about commitment.
bet_sizing.obj4: Plan the three streets with pot geometry instead of guessing each bet.
bet_sizing.fund_p1: Two ideas tie this lesson together. SPR (stack-to-pot ratio) is the ratio between the effective stack and the pot size on the flop: a low SPR means little is left behind (you commit easily); a high SPR means a lot of depth (you have more room to maneuver). Pot geometry is the idea of planning the sizes across the three streets so that, if you bet on all of them, the stack runs out exactly on the river, without needing a strange overbet at the end.
bet_sizing.fund_p2: In practice, the size follows the context: the stack (a tournament calls for smaller opens), position (out of position you charge more), texture (a wet board calls for a bigger bet) and the plan (want to get all-in on the river? start sizing from the flop). Remember: the size is a decision, not a habit.
bet_sizing.c1_p1: The open is the initial preflop raise. At tournament stacks (20 to 50 BB), the standard is small: about 2 to 2.5 BB. Why small? Because you risk fewer chips to steal the blinds, and the fold equity you would gain by opening big does not make up for the extra cost. At deep cash stacks the conversation shifts a bit, but for MTTs the rule is clear: open small and consistent, the same size with your whole range, so as not to give away the strength of your hand.
bet_sizing.c1_key: Tournament open: 2 to 2.5 BB, the same with your whole range. Opening big risks more without gaining proportional fold equity.
bet_sizing.c2_p1: The 3-bet is the re-raise over an open. Here position drives the size. In position (you act after on the postflop), the standard 3-bet is about 3x the open. Out of position (you act before), charge more: about 4x the open. Why the difference? Out of position you play the next streets at a disadvantage, so the larger size pays off: it builds the pot when you have value and denies the villain the chance to call cheaply and outplay you later.
bet_sizing.c2_tip: 3-bet in position: ~3x the open. Out of position: ~4x the open. Squeeze (over an open and a call): add roughly 1x per caller.
bet_sizing.c3_p1: Postflop, the size follows the board texture (the Postflop lesson goes deeper on this). In short: a dry board calls for a small, frequent bet; a wet board calls for a bigger, more selective bet. The table below gathers the standard sizes from this lesson in one place, for you to reference.
bet_sizing.c4_p1: The SPR decides whether you are committed to the hand. With a low SPR (about 2) and a strong hand, like good top pair, so little is left behind that the natural plan is to get the chips in: you are committed and should not fold to a bet. With a high SPR, a lot is left, so even top pair needs caution, because there is a lot to lose on the following streets. Before inflating the pot on the flop, ask: with this SPR, do I want to commit with this hand?
bet_sizing.c4_cap: Pot 20, stacks 40: SPR ~2. With top pair (K-Q on K-8-3), you are committed. The plan is to play for stacks, not to bet and then fold.
bet_sizing.c4_coach: You control the SPR through the preflop size: 3-bets and 4-bets lower the SPR (bigger pots, more commitment on the flop); calls keep the SPR high. Choose the preflop size thinking about how you want to play the flop.
bet_sizing.c5_p1: If your plan is to bet all three streets and get all-in on the river, do not guess each bet: use geometry. The idea is to choose a fixed size relative to the pot (for example, between half pot and 2/3) that, applied on the flop, the turn and the river, spends the stack smoothly until it runs out on the river. That way you extract the maximum without needing a huge, strange overbet at the end that gives the hand away. Planning the pot like a three-step staircase is what separates value taken well from value wasted.
bet_sizing.c5_curio: That is why professionals seem to 'know' the river size: they already planned the staircase from the flop. The river bet is not improvised, it is the last step of a plan that started two streets earlier.
bet_sizing.ex_p1: Situation: 40 BB stacks. The CO opens to 2.5 BB and you have a strong hand on the BTN. 3-bet, and how much? Since you are in position, the standard size is about 3x the open, so ~7.5 BB. This builds the pot with your strong hand, charges dearly those who want to call and keeps you in control on the next street.
bet_sizing.ex_p2: Common mistake: giving a 'min 3-bet' of 5 BB. Why it is bad: 5 BB gives odds that are too good, the villain calls with a ton of hands and you end up with a big pot, out of control, against a wide range. Another mistake: a huge 12 BB 3-bet with no plan, which commits chips for nothing and scares the worse hands out. The right size (~3x) sits in the middle: it charges enough without overdoing it. The size has a reason; it is not a guess.
bet_sizing.cmp_r1_rec: Opens big (3-4 BB) and varies by hand.
bet_sizing.cmp_r1_reg: Opens ~2.5 BB, but sometimes 'protects' by opening bigger.
bet_sizing.cmp_r1_pro: ~2 to 2.5 BB, the same with the whole range.
bet_sizing.cmp_r2_rec: A single size, without looking at position.
bet_sizing.cmp_r2_reg: Adjusts IP/OOP, but gets the squeeze wrong.
bet_sizing.cmp_r2_pro: ~3x IP, ~4x OOP, +1x per caller on the squeeze.
bet_sizing.cmp_r3_rec: Decides each bet on the spot.
bet_sizing.cmp_r3_pro: Plans the geometry of the three streets from the flop.
bet_sizing.e1_m: Using a single size for everything.
bet_sizing.e1_why: Each situation calls for a size; a single one is always wrong somewhere.
bet_sizing.e1_fix: Check the table: open, 3-bet IP/OOP, c-bet by texture.
bet_sizing.e2_m: Opening big in a tournament 'to take it down right away'.
bet_sizing.e2_why: You risk more chips without gaining proportional fold equity.
bet_sizing.e2_fix: Open 2 to 2.5 BB, the same with the whole range.
bet_sizing.e3_why: Gives odds that are too good; the villain calls wide and you lose control.
bet_sizing.e4_m: Ignoring the SPR when inflating the pot on the flop.
bet_sizing.e4_why: A low SPR commits you; a high SPR demands caution even with a good hand.
bet_sizing.e4_fix: Before building the pot, look at the SPR and decide whether you want to commit.
bet_sizing.e5_m: Guessing the size of each street on the spot.
bet_sizing.e5_why: Without a plan, you end up with a strange overbet on the river or without a stack for value.
bet_sizing.e5_fix: Plan the geometry: one size per street that spends the stack by the river.
bet_sizing.e6_m: Varying the size by the strength of the hand.
bet_sizing.e6_why: Betting big with value and small with a bluff gives away your hand.
bet_sizing.e6_fix: Use the same size with value and bluff in the same spot; let the texture decide.
bet_sizing.chk1: Is it preflop or postflop? What is the situation (open, 3-bet, c-bet)?
bet_sizing.chk2: Am I in position or out of it? (out means bigger)
bet_sizing.chk3: What is the board texture? (dry = small, wet = bigger)
bet_sizing.chk4: What is the SPR? Do I want to commit with this hand?
bet_sizing.chk5: If the plan is to bet all 3 streets, does today's size fit the staircase to the river?
bet_sizing.drill_sub: Open, 3-bet and SPR spots on what you just learned. Every correct answer is worth XP.
bet_sizing.t1: The size communicates and charges; use reasoning, not a single size.
bet_sizing.t2: Open ~2 to 2.5 BB; 3-bet ~3x in position, ~4x out of position.
bet_sizing.t3: Postflop follows the texture: dry small, wet bigger.
bet_sizing.t4: Think about SPR and plan the geometry of the three streets.
bet_sizing.g1_def: The initial preflop raise, when no one has entered the pot before you.
bet_sizing.g2_def: The re-raise over an open (the third bet in the sequence).
bet_sizing.g3_def: Stack-to-pot ratio: the ratio between the effective stack and the pot on the flop.
bet_sizing.g4_def: Planning the street sizes to spend the stack smoothly until the river.
bet_sizing.g5_def: A 3-bet over an open and one or more callers; charges more per caller.
bet_sizing.g6_def: When the SPR is so low that the right play is to get the chips in, not to give up to a bet.
bet_sizing.gl_intro: The theory above turns into practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads the sizes of your bets and shows where your sizing is costing you EV.
bet_sizing.gl1: Checks the size of your open by position and flags opens that are too big.
bet_sizing.gl2: Evaluates the 3-bet size (in position vs out of position) against the standard.
bet_sizing.gl3: Compares your c-bet with the node's ideal size by texture.
bet_sizing.gl4: Flags commitment spots from a poorly evaluated SPR.
bet_sizing.gl5: Generates sizing drills from your own hands.
bet_sizing.next_sub: Continue with the c-bet, textures and barrel lesson.

postflop.title: Postflop: c-bet, barrel and textures
postflop.subtitle: The complete lesson on poker's most common bet: when to c-bet, how to read dry and wet boards, what size to use and when to keep betting on the turn. With table examples and drills.
postflop.intro_p1: The flop is where most of poker's money is won and lost. And the most common decision of all is the continuation bet (c-bet): you opened preflop, you became the aggressor, and now you have to decide whether to bet the flop or not. Doing this well, betting the right times, on the right boards and with the right size, is one of the skills that most separates winners from losers.
postflop.intro_p2: The classic beginner mistake is to c-bet every time, at the same size, on any board. That leaks chips: on some flops you bet too much, on others too little, and almost always with the wrong size. In this lesson you will learn to read the board (dry versus wet), understand who has the advantage, choose the size and decide when to keep betting on the turn.
postflop.intro_note: We will cover the most common case: you are the preflop aggressor, heads-up, and you decide what to do on the flop and the turn. The same ideas extend to multiway pots, where you bet even less (see the Multiway lesson).
postflop.obj1: Understand what a c-bet is and why the preflop aggressor usually has the advantage on the flop.
postflop.obj2: Read the board texture: dry versus wet, and what each one calls for.
postflop.obj3: Choose the right size for your bet based on the board.
postflop.obj4: Decide when to keep betting on the turn (barrel) and when to slow down.
postflop.fund_p1: A c-bet (continuation bet) is the bet the preflop aggressor makes on the flop, continuing the story of strength. Range advantage is the idea that, on a given board, one player has more strong hands than the other. Whoever opened the pot usually has more high cards and big pairs, so on many flops it is the opener who has the advantage. Whoever has the range advantage can bet more often.
postflop.fund_p2: Texture is how dangerous the board is. A dry flop (scattered cards, different suits, like K-7-2) has few draws and favors whoever has high cards. A wet flop (connected cards and/or the same suit, like 9-8-7 of hearts) creates many pairs, straights and draws, and helps whoever defended. Reading the texture is the first step of every postflop decision.
postflop.c1_p1: Before betting, ask: whose advantage is it on this board? Since you opened the pot, your range has more Aces, Kings and high pairs. On a high-card flop (A-K-4, K-7-2), that advantage is yours: you hit the board more often than the villain, who most of the time only called with medium cards. When the advantage is yours, you can bet often, even without having hit anything, because your story is credible and the villain has many weak hands to fold.
postflop.c1_key: Whoever has the range advantage bets more. As the preflop aggressor on a high-card board, that advantage is almost always yours: c-bet often.
postflop.c2_title: Chapter 2: dry board versus wet board
postflop.c2_p1: On a dry board, like K-7-2 of different suits, there are almost no draws. The villain has few hands that want to continue, and you dominate with high cards. Here the standard play is a small and frequent c-bet: you profit cheaply, denying the equity of his high cards and winning many pots without a showdown.
postflop.c2_cap_dry: Dry board K-7-2 (rainbow). You are the aggressor in position with A-Q. A small c-bet with almost your entire range is the standard.
postflop.c2_p2: On a wet board, like 9-8-7 with two cards of the same suit, everything changes. Now the villain has many pairs, two pairs, straights and flush draws. Your advantage disappears or even flips. Here you bet less often and more selectively: bigger with strong value and good draws, and you check more with the hands that do not want to face a raise or build a pot.
postflop.c2_cap_wet: Wet board 9-8-7 (two suits). Even with a strong pair, the approach is more selective: protect with value and check more with the rest.
postflop.c3_p1: The size communicates and charges. On a dry board, use a small size (around 1/3 of the pot): you do not need to bet big because the villain has little to call with, and the small size lets you bet cheaply with your whole range. On a wet board, use a bigger size (half pot or more) that is more polarized: you want to charge the draws a high price when you have value, and the bigger size does that job. Rule of thumb: a dry board calls for a small bet, a wet board calls for a bigger bet.
postflop.c3_tip: Dry: ~1/3 pot with almost everything. Wet: half pot or more, but less often. The size follows the texture, not your mood.
postflop.c4_p1: You c-bet the flop and the villain called. The turn comes: do I bet again (barrel) or slow down? The right question is: does this card help my range more or his? High cards (A, K, Q) generally hit the opener's range more, so they are great for continuing to bet. Low cards that connect the board or complete draws help whoever defended. There, your bluff loses value and the right move is to slow down, bet less and control the pot. Good barrels are not courage, they are cards that improve your story.
postflop.c4_coach: Before barreling, look at the turn and ask: 'is this card more mine or more his?'. If it is yours (a high card), keep betting. If it is his (a card that connects), take a breath and control the pot.
postflop.c5_p1: A c-bet is not mandatory. There are boards where the advantage belongs to the villain (low, connected flops like 7-6-5, which hit the caller's range more), and there the right move is to check a good part of your range, including decent hands, so you do not leak chips betting into an unfavorable field. Checking also protects your medium hands: you control the pot and avoid facing a raise. Knowing how to check is as important as knowing how to bet.
postflop.c5_warn: C-betting every flop, always, is a classic leak. On boards that favor the villain, betting too much just donates chips. Not every flop is yours.
postflop.ex_p1: Situation: you open on the BTN, the BB calls. Two go to the flop. Case 1, dry board K-7-2 rainbow: even with a weak hand like A-Q, the standard play is a small c-bet. Why: the board hits your aggressor range a lot and almost none of the BB's, who called with medium cards. You profit cheaply and win many pots right away.
postflop.ex_p2: Case 2, wet board 9-8-7 of two suits: now, even with a good hand, the approach changes. This flop hits the BB's range (straights, two pairs, draws), so you bet less often and more selectively: bigger when you have strong value, check with a good part of the rest. Common mistake: c-betting small with everything here, as if it were the dry board, and getting called or raised by the villain's many strong hands. The texture changes the decision, the hand alone does not.
postflop.cmp_r1_rec: C-bets almost always, on any board.
postflop.cmp_r1_reg: Adjusts a little, but still bets too much on bad boards.
postflop.cmp_r1_pro: A lot on the dry, selective on the wet, checks what is the villain's.
postflop.cmp_r2_rec: One size only, usually too big.
postflop.cmp_r2_pro: Small on the dry, bigger and polarized on the wet.
postflop.cmp_r3_rec: Fires or gives up without looking at the card.
postflop.cmp_r3_reg: Barrels out of habit, without reading the turn.
postflop.cmp_r3_pro: Barrels on the cards that help his range, slows down on the villain's.
postflop.e1_why: On boards that favor the villain, betting just donates chips.
postflop.e1_fix: Read the texture: a lot on the dry, selective on the wet, check what is his.
postflop.e2_why: A dry board calls for small, a wet one calls for bigger. A single size is always wrong somewhere.
postflop.e2_fix: Small (~1/3) on the dry, half pot or more on the wet.
postflop.e3_m: Betting big on a dry board 'to protect'.
postflop.e3_why: The villain has little to call with, the big bet only isolates the hands that beat you.
postflop.e3_fix: On the dry, small and frequent extracts more and risks less.
postflop.e4_m: Barreling the turn without looking at the card.
postflop.e4_why: Cards that help the villain make your bluff lose value.
postflop.e4_fix: Ask whether the turn is more yours or his before betting again.
postflop.e5_why: On the villain's boards, betting everything leaks, and you end up with no medium hands in your check.
postflop.e5_fix: Include checks in your range, mainly on low, connected flops.
postflop.e6_m: Copying the same line in a multiway pot.
postflop.e6_why: With 3 or more players the c-bet drops a lot, your range advantage gets diluted.
postflop.e6_fix: Bet much less in multiway (see the Multiway lesson).
postflop.chk1: Am I the preflop aggressor? Is the story of strength mine?
postflop.chk2: Is the board dry or wet? Whose range advantage is it?
postflop.chk3: If I am going to bet, does the size follow the texture (small on the dry, bigger on the wet)?
postflop.chk4: Is this heads-up or multiway? Multiway means betting much less.
postflop.chk5: On the turn: does the card help my range more or the villain's?
postflop.drill_sub: C-bet, texture and barrel spots on what you just learned. Every correct answer is worth XP.
postflop.t1: The c-bet is the most common decision; doing it well separates winners from losers.
postflop.t2: Dry board: bet small and frequent. Wet: less and selective.
postflop.t3: The size follows the texture: small on the dry, bigger on the wet.
postflop.t4: On the turn, barrel the cards that help your range, slow down on the ones that help the villain.
postflop.g1_def: Continuation bet: the preflop aggressor's bet on the flop, continuing the story.
postflop.g2_def: Having more strong hands than the opponent on a given board; it lets you bet more.
postflop.g3_def: A flop of scattered cards and different suits (few draws), favors high cards.
postflop.g4_def: A connected flop and/or of the same suit, full of draws; favors whoever defended.
postflop.g5_def: Continuing to bet on the next street (double barrel on the turn, triple on the river).
postflop.g6_def: A betting range made of very strong hands and bluffs, with little in the middle.
postflop.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads your hands and shows where your c-bet is leaking EV, without you having to look for it.
postflop.gl1: Measures your c-bet frequency by position and by board type.
postflop.gl2: Splits your performance across dry versus connected boards.
postflop.gl3: Points out the EV lost on inadequate c-bets (wrong board or wrong size).
