multiway.title: Pot with 3+ players: why everything changes
multiway.subtitle: A complete lesson on multiway pots: why the math turns inside out and what to do with bluffing, value, bet size and position. With table examples and a drill.
multiway.intro_p1: Most of what is taught in poker starts from a heads-up pot: you against a single opponent. But in the practice of a tournament, many pots have three, four or more players seeing the flop, especially at medium stacks and against recreational players, who love to call to see. Those are multiway pots, and in them the math you learned for heads-up simply stops holding.
multiway.intro_p2: Understanding multiway matters because it is where a lot of EV leaks without you noticing. The classic beginner mistake is to play the 4-player pot with the same mindset as the 2-player pot: bluffing the same, betting the same size, calling with the same hands. Each of those transfers costs chips. In this lesson you will see, step by step, what changes and why, so you make better decisions whenever the table fills up.
multiway.intro_note: Multiway pots are more common at deep stacks, at tables with many limps and calls, in recreational fields and in blind spots against several limpers. The looser the field, the more multiway you will play, and the more this lesson is worth.
multiway.obj1: Explain why multiway changes the math of the pot, not just memorize the rule.
multiway.obj2: Know when bluffing is worth it and when it is money thrown away with several opponents.
multiway.obj3: Choose which hands to continue with and the right size for your bet in multiway.
multiway.obj4: Recognize the middle seat and adjust your posture so you do not leak chips.
multiway.fund_p1: Before moving on, three simple ideas. Equity is your share of the chance to win the pot if the hand went to showdown right now; with a high card against a random opponent you have plenty of equity, but against three hands that continued your share shrinks, because there are more players with a bigger chance of having hit something. Range is the set of hands a player can have on that line; the more players continue, the more the ranges against you 'add up'.
multiway.fund_p2: The central idea of multiway is shared defense: for you to profit betting as a bluff, everyone has to give up; to hold you off, it is enough for just ONE to have a hand. In other words, the responsibility of calling you is split among the opponents, but the responsibility of making your bluff work is entirely yours, against all of them. That is why, with more players, bluffing is worth less and value needs to be stronger. Keep the phrase: in multiway, the burden of the bluff is yours; the burden of calling you is shared.
multiway.c1_p1: Bluffing is betting hoping the opponent gives up. Against one person, you can steal a lot of pots, because most of the time they also did not hit anything. Against two or three, the math changes: you need all of them to give up at the same time. If each one folds 60% of the time, against one the bluff goes through 60%; against three, it goes through only 0.6 x 0.6 x 0.6, about 22%. The chance of someone calling you skyrockets.
multiway.c1_p2: In practice: in a multiway pot, a pure bluff (a hand with no pair and no draw) almost never turns a profit. If you have no way to improve, the right move is almost always to give up cheaply (check or fold) instead of burning chips trying to push out three people. A multiway bluff only makes sense with a strong draw alongside it (semi-bluff), when you can still win the pot by hitting your hand.
multiway.scene1_cap: 3-way pot on the flop. You (BTN) have A-K of the same suit as two cards on the board: a draw to the best flush. That is a semi-bluff, not a pure bluff.
multiway.c1_warn: A pure bluff against 3 or more opponents is practically zero. Save your chips for when you have a made hand or a strong draw. Wanting to 'represent strength' against three is the fastest way to donate your stack.
multiway.c2_title: Chapter 2: tighten your value, only strong hands continue
multiway.c2_p1: On the other side, the hands you continue with also change. A medium pair that would easily call against one player becomes a fold against three, because now it needs to be ahead of several hands at the same time, and someone almost always has a better pair, top pair or a draw. The bar rises: continue with truly strong hands and with draws that make the best hands (nut draws, like the draw to the highest flush). Drop the mediocre hands; in multiway they call to lose.
multiway.cards_cap: A-K of the same suit, on a flop with two cards of that suit, is a draw to the best flush: continue. A pair of 8s (below the board) that would hold against one opponent becomes a fold against several.
multiway.c2_coach: Before calling in multiway, ask: 'how many hands do I need to be ahead of at the same time?'. Against three, the answer is 'all three'. That alone throws half of your marginal hands in the trash, and that is fine.
multiway.c3_p1: With more players in the pot, you do not need to bet so big. The pot is already bigger (more players put in chips) and there is more chance of someone calling, so a smaller bet already does the job of extracting value and charging the draws. Besides, betting very big without an almost unbeatable hand is a trap: when one of the three calls your overbet, it is almost always because they have you beat. Smaller sizes keep you safe and still build the pot when you have real value.
multiway.c3_tip: Rule of thumb: in multiway, without the nuts, prefer bets of half pot or less. Leave overbets for when you have the hand that beats everyone.
multiway.c4_title: Chapter 4: the middle seat is the worst spot
multiway.c4_p1: There is one specific spot that bleeds chips: being in the middle. Imagine someone bet before you and there are still players to act after. You are sandwiched: if you call, you can still face a raise from those behind, and you are up against two unknown hands at the same time. You have the worst information at the table and the biggest risk. In this seat, play tight: continue only with hands that can take pressure from both sides and avoid calling loose just out of curiosity.
multiway.scene5_cap: Opponent A bet, you are in the middle (HJ) and Opponent B is still to act. Sandwiched: continue with fewer hands than in a normal pot.
multiway.c4_curio: This seat has a nickname in players' jargon: 'the monkey in the middle'. It is the position where people make the most mistakes by calling too much, precisely because we hate folding after already putting in chips.
multiway.ex_p1: Let's put it all together in a real spot. Situation: tournament, stacks of ~40 BB. The player in early position (MP) opens, you call in the CO with 9-9 and the BTN also calls. Three go to the flop A-T-6. MP bets half the pot. What is your decision?
multiway.ex_cap: Three players on the flop A-T-6. You have 9-9 (a pair below the Ace and the Ten) and you face a bet from the opener.
multiway.ex_p2: Correct decision: fold. Why: the board has an Ace and a Ten, cards that the range of the opener and the caller hit a lot. Your 9-9 is behind any Ace, any Ten and draws that are still on you. Against one opponent, calling once would be debatable; against two, you need to be ahead of both at the same time, and you almost never are. Incorrect decision: calling 'to see the turn'. That leaks chips: you call now and probably again, only to give up in the end or lose a big pot. The recreational player calls out of curiosity; the professional lets go and saves the chips for a better spot.
multiway.cmp_r1_rec: Bluffs the same as heads-up, tries to push everyone out.
multiway.cmp_r1_reg: Bluffs less, but still insists with air sometimes.
multiway.cmp_r1_pro: Only semi-bluffs with a strong draw; a pure bluff is ~zero.
multiway.cmp_r2_rec: Calls loose, 'to see', with medium pairs and high cards.
multiway.cmp_r2_reg: Tightens a little, but still calls marginals out of habit.
multiway.cmp_r2_pro: Continues only with a strong hand and nut draws; lets the rest go.
multiway.cmp_r3_pro: Smaller without the nuts; big only with the hand that beats everyone.
multiway.e1_m: Pure bluffing against 3 or more players.
multiway.e1_why: Everyone has to fold at the same time, and the chance of someone calling is high.
multiway.e1_fix: Without a strong draw, give up cheaply. A multiway bluff only as a semi-bluff.
multiway.e2_m: Calling with a medium pair 'to see the turn' in multiway.
multiway.e2_why: You need to be ahead of several hands; you are almost always already behind.
multiway.e2_fix: Let go of the marginals. Continue only with hands that beat several ranges.
multiway.e3_m: Betting big or overbetting without the nuts.
multiway.e3_why: Whoever calls a big multiway bet usually has you beat.
multiway.e3_fix: Cut the size: half pot or less without the max hand.
multiway.e4_m: Playing the middle seat as if you were the last to act.
multiway.e4_why: You can still face a raise from those behind and you have the worst information.
multiway.e4_fix: Tighten your range in the middle; call only with hands that hold both sides.
multiway.e5_m: Treating every pot as if it were heads-up.
multiway.e5_why: The 1-versus-1 math does not hold when there are 3 or 4 hands alive.
multiway.e5_fix: Before acting, count the active players and adjust bluff, value and sizing.
multiway.e6_m: Insisting because you already put in chips (the sunk cost).
multiway.e6_why: Chips in the pot are no longer yours; the right decision looks forward.
multiway.e6_fix: Decide by the EV from here on. Folding a pot you 'already invested in' is normal.
multiway.chk1: How many players are still alive in the hand?
multiway.chk2: Does my hand beat several ranges at the same time, or just one?
multiway.chk3: If I bet, is it value or a bluff? A pure bluff against several is almost never.
multiway.chk4: Is my size smaller because I do not have the nuts?
multiway.chk5: Am I in the middle? If so, who can raise me from behind?
multiway.drill_sub: A few quick spots on what you just learned. Every correct answer is worth XP.
multiway.t1: Multiway is a different game: for a play to work, it has to get past everyone.
multiway.t2: A pure bluff plummets; continue only with strong hands and nut draws.
multiway.t3: Bet smaller without the nuts; a multiway overbet is a trap.
multiway.t4: In the middle seat, tighten up: worst information and risk of a raise behind.
multiway.g1_def: A pot with three or more players still in the hand.
multiway.g2_def: A pot with only two players, one against the other.
multiway.g3_def: Your share of the chance to win the pot if the hand went to showdown right now.
multiway.g4_def: A draw that, when completed, makes the best possible hand (for example, the highest flush).
multiway.g5_def: A bet with a hand that is not yet the best, but has a strong draw to become it.
multiway.g6_def: The position between a player who already acted and others who are still to act; the hardest one.
multiway.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads your imported hands and shows where you are leaking EV in multiway pots, without you having to look for it.
multiway.gl1: Detects when the pot is multiway and marks those spots with the ≈ Approximation tag (the solver is heads-up).
multiway.gl2: Measures your bluff frequency and continuation in pots with several players.
multiway.gl3: Points out the hands where you called loose or bet big without enough value.
multiway.gl4: Compares your multiway behavior with that of winning players at the same buy-in level.
multiway.gl5: Generates personalized drills from your own hands, so you can practice where you go wrong.
multiway.next_sub: Take this to the Drill and see how you play the multiway pots from your own game.

icm.title: ICM: why survival earns a premium
icm.subtitle: The complete ICM lesson: why chips change value in a tournament, the risk premium, when ICM kicks in, the bubble and the final table. With table examples and training.
icm.intro_p1: In cash games every chip is worth real money, always the same amount. In a tournament, it is not. You traded money for chips at the entry, but you only get money back if you finish in a paid position. This changes everything: the right decision is no longer 'win the most chips', it becomes 'maximize your expected money'. The model that translates chips into expected money is called ICM (Independent Chip Model), and it is what separates those who understand tournaments from those who just play chips.
icm.intro_p2: Ignoring ICM is one of the most expensive leaks in MTTs, and the most silent one, because each mistake looks small. Calling a 'chip-profitable' all-in on the bubble, battling the other big stack with no need, folding too much when you are the short who needs chips: all of that is ICM applied poorly. In this lesson you will understand why, and gain practical rules for the stages where money is on the line.
icm.intro_note: ICM weighs most near pay jumps: the bubble (the last player out before the money), the entry into the money and, at full strength, the final table, where each position pays much more than the previous one.
icm.obj1: Explain why a chip won is worth less than a chip lost in a tournament.
icm.obj2: Understand the risk premium: why you need more equity to risk your stack near the money.
icm.obj3: Know when ICM kicks in and how the bubble and final table change your decisions.
icm.obj4: Adjust your play according to your stack: the big stack pressures, the short chooses well.
icm.fund_p1: Start with the central idea: in a tournament chips are not worth money linearly. Doubling your stack does not double your expected prize, because the maximum prize is capped (first place) and you split the rest with the other survivors. That is why the chip you win brings you closer to the prize bit by bit, but the chip you lose, when it busts you, costs everything. Winning and losing are not symmetrical, and that asymmetry is the heart of ICM.
icm.fund_p2: From that comes the risk premium: a risk premium you demand before putting your stack at stake. In pure chips (we call it chipEV), a decision is good if it wins chips in the long run. In tournament value (the $EV), the same decision can be bad if the risk of busting costs more than the extra chips are worth. Near the money, you start to demand more certainty (more equity) to call an all-in or to commit. Remember: chipEV rules early; $EV rules near the prize.
icm.c1_p1: Imagine a tournament with 4 players and 3 paid, and you are the biggest stack by far. If you risk everything in a spot that wins a tiny bit of chips in the long run and you lose, you go out in 4th and take zero, even having been the leader. If you simply wait, you probably reach the money by letting the others battle. In this scenario, doubling your chips barely changes your prize, but busting destroys it completely.
icm.c1_p2: The lesson: sometimes the right play is to give up a chip profit to protect your place in the prize pool. It is not cowardice, it is math: you are maximizing money, not chips. The more uneven the prize distribution and the closer to the jump, the stronger this effect.
icm.c1_key: Chips won are worth less than chips lost. The bigger the next pay jump and the deeper your stack for survival, the more you should avoid unnecessary risks.
icm.c2_p1: In practice, the risk premium shows up as an extra equity requirement. A call that would need, say, 40% chance to win counting only chips can start to require 50% or more near the money. The difference is the price of surviving. Look at the scene: you have a medium stack on the bubble, the chip leader shoves over the top, and you hold a marginal hand.
icm.c2_cap: Bubble: the chip leader (BTN) went all-in, you (BB, medium stack) have A-J and the short (SB) is about to bust. In chips, calling can be slightly profitable; in tournament value, it is almost always a fold.
icm.c2_p2: Why fold? If you call and lose, you go out before locking up the money, while the short probably busts first if you just wait. You do not need this confrontation: your place in the prize is already almost secured. Calling here trades a safe position for a risky bet that, in money terms, is negative, even while being positive in chips.
icm.c2_warn: The classic mistake is to look only at pot odds and say 'I have the odds to call'. Near the money, pot odds are not enough: add the risk premium. The hand needs to be well ahead, not just a little ahead.
icm.c3_p1: ICM does not only kick in on the bubble. It starts to weigh when a good part of the field is already eliminated (around half) and grows until the final table, where it is at its maximum. Early in the tournament, with many players and distant prizes, accumulating chips dominates and tightening out of fear is what becomes a leak. As the field shrinks and the pay jumps approach, the ICM brake tightens. Knowing which stage you are in is half the decision.
icm.c3_tip: Early, far from the money: play for chips, accumulate. Near half the field: start thinking twice before risking your stack. Bubble and final table: survival is worth gold, be selective.
icm.c4_title: Chapter 4: MDF dies on the bubble
icm.c4_p1: There is a postflop rule called minimum defense frequency (MDF): it tells you how much you need to continue so as not to be exploited by whoever bets. The catch is that MDF assumes losing the pot only costs chips. On the bubble, losing can mean being eliminated with no prize, so the cost of defending and being wrong skyrockets. Counter-intuitive result: near the money you defend less, not more, even against someone who bluffs a lot. Defending wide on the bubble, 'so I am not robbed', is one of the most expensive leaks in the tournament.
icm.c4_coach: On the bubble, let the villain steal the small pots. Surviving the pay jump is worth much more than being right in a small pot. You recover those blinds easily once the money is reached; your tournament, you do not.
icm.c5_title: Chapter 5: the big stack pressures, the short chooses well
icm.c5_p1: ICM also depends on who you are at the table. As the big stack on the bubble or the final table, you have the lowest risk premium: busting does not eliminate you, and you force the medium stacks (who have a lot to lose) to fold too much. This is the time to pressure, steal and apply constant pressure. As the medium stack, you are the most pressured: tighten up, avoid dueling with whoever covers you. As the short, your risk premium disappears (you are already almost out): pick your moment well and commit when you have a hand, because waiting too long also kills you.
icm.c5_cap: You are the big stack (BTN, 60 BB) against a short (BB, 5 BB) and a medium (CO). Near the money, you are the one applying the pressure.
icm.ex_p1: Situation: tournament bubble, 4 players left and 3 get paid. You have a medium stack (15 BB) in the BB with A-J offsuit. The chip leader (60 BB) goes all-in from the BTN and the short (5 BB) is still in the SB. The pot odds say A-J is 'almost a call'. What do you do?
icm.ex_p2: Correct decision: fold. Why: A-J against the chip leader's shove range is, at best, a little ahead or even behind, and you do not need this risk. If you fold, the short (5 BB) probably busts before you, and you lock up the pay jump without risking anything. Calling trades an almost safe position for a coin flip that, in money terms, is negative. Incorrect decision: call 'because I have the odds'. That ignores the risk premium: in chips the call may break even, but in $EV it loses, because busting on the bubble is the worst possible result. The pro folds and lets the short fall.
icm.cmp_r1_rec: Calls 'because I have odds', ignores ICM.
icm.cmp_r1_reg: Tightens a bit, but still calls marginals sometimes.
icm.cmp_r1_pro: Demands a hand well ahead; folds spots +EV in chips but -$EV.
icm.cmp_r2_rec: Defends wide so as 'not to be robbed'.
icm.cmp_r2_reg: Defends near the MDF, without adjusting for ICM.
icm.cmp_r2_pro: Over-folds: lets the small pots go and protects the jump.
icm.cmp_r3_rec: Plays the same, whether big or short.
icm.cmp_r3_reg: Adjusts little according to the stack.
icm.cmp_r3_pro: Big pressures; medium tightens; short picks the moment.
icm.e1_m: Calling an all-in on the bubble 'because I have pot odds'.
icm.e1_why: Pot odds ignore the risk premium; busting on the bubble is the worst result.
icm.e1_fix: Add ICM: only call with a hand well ahead, not just a little ahead.
icm.e2_m: Defending wide on the bubble so as not to be robbed.
icm.e2_why: MDF assumes cost only in chips; on the bubble busting costs the tournament.
icm.e2_fix: Over-fold: let the small pots go and protect the pay jump.
icm.e3_m: Dueling with the other big stack with no need.
icm.e3_why: You risk a lot of money in a confrontation that does not need to happen.
icm.e3_fix: As the big stack, pressure the mediums; avoid fighting whoever covers you.
icm.e4_m: Tightening too much as a short who needs chips.
icm.e4_why: Waiting until you almost blind out also kills you; your risk premium is already low.
icm.e4_fix: Pick your moment and commit with a hand before you turn to dust.
icm.e5_m: Playing the final table as if it were the middle of the tournament.
icm.e5_why: At the final table each pay jump is huge; ICM is at its maximum.
icm.e5_fix: Be more selective; surviving each elimination is worth a lot.
icm.e6_m: Treating chipEV as if it were always $EV.
icm.e6_why: Near the money, winning chips is not the same as winning money.
icm.e6_fix: Ask whether the decision is good in MONEY, not just in chips.
icm.chk1: What stage am I in? Far from the money, the bubble or the final table?
icm.chk2: What is the next pay jump and how much does it weigh?
icm.chk3: Am I big, medium or short? That changes my risk premium.
icm.chk4: Is my hand WELL ahead, or just a little (simple pot odds)?
icm.chk5: Is there a shorter stack that probably busts before me?
icm.drill_sub: ICM and bubble spots on what you just learned. Each correct answer is worth XP.
icm.t1: Chips won are worth less than chips lost; the goal is money, not chips.
icm.t2: Near the money, demand the risk premium: a hand well ahead to risk the stack.
icm.t3: ICM kicks in around half the field and is at its maximum at the final table.
icm.t4: The big stack pressures, the medium tightens, the short picks the moment.
icm.g1_def: Independent Chip Model: translates your chip stack into expected money in the tournament.
icm.g2_def: Extra equity you demand to risk your stack near a pay jump.
icm.g3_def: chipEV measures the decision in chips; $EV measures it in money. Near the prize, $EV is what counts.
icm.g4_def: The stage right before the money; the next player out wins nothing.
icm.g5_def: Pay jump: the prize difference between one position and the next.
icm.g6_def: Minimum defense: how much you need to continue so as not to be exploited. It drops under ICM.
icm.gl_intro: The theory above becomes practice inside the platform. GrindLab reads your hands and shows where ICM is costing you money, without you having to look for it.
icm.gl1: The Collapse Under Pressure card compares your decision quality without ICM and under high ICM.
icm.gl2: It detects correct tightening under ICM and does not flag it as a mistake (the ≈ chipEV approximation badge).
icm.gl3: It points out calls on the bubble that are +EV in chips but negative in money.
icm.gl4: It measures your BB defense near the money and flags over-defense.
icm.gl5: It generates bubble drills from your own ICM spots.
icm.next_sub: Continue the advanced track with the lesson on pots with 3 or more players.

exploits.title: Exploits & Reads: how to exploit each type of player
exploits.subtitle: GTO is the base that never loses. But real profit comes from reading the type of villain and exploiting the specific mistake of each one. Learn the archetypes and the right adjustment for each.
exploits.intro_p1: Playing GTO (balanced) makes you unbeatable: nobody exploits you. But GTO doesn't attack the villain's mistakes, it only defends against yours. Against real players, who make plenty of mistakes, real profit comes from deviating from balance to punish the specific error of each one. That is exploitative play, and it starts with one question: what type of player is this?
exploits.intro_p2: In this lesson you'll learn to recognize the most common archetypes (calling station, nit, aggressor) and the right adjustment for each one. The idea is simple and powerful: every archetype has a characteristic mistake, and the profitable play is to do the opposite of that mistake. Against someone who won't fold, you stop bluffing; against someone who folds too much, you bluff more; against someone who bluffs too much, you call more.
exploits.intro_note: GTO is the floor that never loses; exploit is the ceiling that wins more. Start from balance (you've learned MDF, ranges, sizing) and, once you have a read on the villain, deviate to attack their mistake. Without a read, go back to GTO.
exploits.obj1: Understand the difference between playing GTO and playing exploitatively.
exploits.obj3: Apply the right adjustment for the mistake of each archetype.
exploits.obj4: Know when not to deviate (lack of sample, balanced player).
exploits.fund_p1: An archetype is a pattern of mistakes. The calling station calls too much and hardly ever folds; the nit plays super tight and folds too much; the LAG or maniac is aggressive and bluffs too much; the whale or recreational calls and bets with no criteria. Each one errs in a different way, and the adjustment is always to do the opposite of their mistake. The table below sums up the three main types plus the recreational.
exploits.fund_p2: The golden rule of exploit: don't bluff someone who won't fold; don't call someone who won't bluff; attack someone who folds too much. Everything else is detail. You don't need a perfect read; you need to know which way the villain errs and push your strategy in that direction. And remember: exploit has a cost, you give up balance, so only deviate when the read is reliable.
exploits.tbl_r1_c: Zero bluffs; thin and bigger value
exploits.tbl_r2_c: Bluff and steal more; respect their bets
exploits.tbl_r4_b: Calls and bets with no criteria
exploits.tbl_r4_c: Heavy value, few bluffs, no fancy play
exploits.c1_p1: The calling station is the most profitable villain and the one the beginner exploits worst. Their mistake is calling too much: they hardly fold anything. The consequence is direct: bluffing against them is throwing money away, because they never give up. On the other hand, they call with a ton of worse hands, so your value hand is worth much more: bet bigger and thinner. That top pair with a medium kicker, which you'd check against a reg, here you bet for value three streets. Heavy value, zero bluffs.
exploits.c1_key_title: The golden rule of the station
exploits.c1_key: Vs calling station: don't bluff (they won't fold) and bet for value with more hands and bigger (they call with trash). Their mistake is your gold mine.
exploits.c2_p1: The nit is the opposite: plays super tight and folds too much. The adjustment has two sides. When you take the initiative, bluff and steal more: they give up blinds and small pots easily, so cheap bluffs and steals profit. But there's a second side that the beginner forgets: when the nit bets, it's real value, because they hardly ever bluff. So respect their bets, fold the marginal hands you'd call against a normal player.
exploits.c2_tip: Vs nit: bluff and steal more when THEY haven't bet; but when they bet strong, believe it and fold your marginals. They're not bluffing.
exploits.c3_p1: The aggressor bluffs and bets too much. The beginner's instinct is to run, to fold to avoid getting exposed, and that's exactly the mistake. If they bluff too much, your medium hands turn into bluff-catchers: call wider and don't over-fold to their pressure. Let them bluff into your hand instead of handing over the pot. A middle pair you'd fold to a reg's bet becomes an easy call against a maniac. Their aggression is the gift; your job is to call.
exploits.c3_coach: Against an aggressor, the courage to call is the exploit. They're giving you money every time they bluff; folding too much is refusing the gift. Adjust your calling threshold downward.
exploits.c4_p1: Exploit has a cost: by deviating from GTO, you become unbalanced and exploitable back. That's why there are two cautions. First, sample: don't label a villain off 5 hands; a couple of calls doesn't make a station. Without enough data, play GTO. Second, the balanced player (a good reg) has no obvious mistake to attack; against them, the exploit disappears and you go back to balance. Exploit is for punishing mistakes; without a clear mistake, there's nothing to punish.
exploits.c4_warn: Don't invent reads. Labeling everyone a station after one call makes you bluff too much and get punished. Demand a sample, and against a good balanced player, play GTO instead of forcing an exploit that isn't there.
exploits.ex_p1: Situation: river, you have top pair with a weak kicker, a medium-value hand. Against a calling station: bet for value, even bigger than normal, because they call with worse pairs and Ace-high. What would be a check against a reg becomes a value bet against them. Against a nit who bet strong: fold; the nit doesn't bluff, so your weak top pair is behind.
exploits.ex_p2: Against a maniac who shoved all-in: call. They bluff too much, so your weak top pair becomes a bluff-catcher and beats all of their bluffs. The same hand, three villains, three opposite actions. That's the heart of exploit: the hand doesn't change, the villain decides the play. Common mistake: playing the hand the same against all three, betting small 'by default'. Against the station you left value on the table; against the nit you called and lost; against the maniac you folded a winner.
exploits.cmp_r1_rec: Doesn't pay attention to the type of player.
exploits.cmp_r1_reg: Notices the extremes, but adjusts little.
exploits.cmp_r1_pro: Classifies the archetype and deviates hard.
exploits.cmp_r2_reg: Bluffs less vs station, but out of habit.
exploits.cmp_r2_pro: Zero vs station, plenty vs nit, calibrated by profile.
exploits.cmp_r3_pro: Calls wide vs maniac, folds marginals vs nit.
exploits.e1_why: They won't fold; the bluff is money thrown away.
exploits.e1_fix: Vs station: zero bluffs, value only (and bigger).
exploits.e2_m: Calling a nit's bets with marginal hands.
exploits.e2_why: The nit hardly ever bluffs; their bet is value.
exploits.e2_fix: Respect the nit's bets; fold your marginals.
exploits.e3_m: Folding too much against a maniac.
exploits.e3_why: They bluff too much; folding refuses the gift.
exploits.e3_fix: Call wider; your medium hands become bluff-catchers.
exploits.e4_why: One call doesn't make a station; a wrong read gets you punished.
exploits.e4_fix: Demand a sample before deviating from GTO.
exploits.e5_m: Forcing an exploit against a good reg.
exploits.e5_why: Without a clear mistake, the deviation only leaves you exploitable.
exploits.e5_fix: Against a balanced player, go back to GTO.
exploits.e6_m: Weak value bet against someone who calls too much.
exploits.e6_why: Against a station you leave a lot of value on the table by betting small.
exploits.e6_fix: Vs station, fatten the value: bigger and thinner.
exploits.chk1: What type of player is this? (station, nit, aggressor, reg)
exploits.chk2: Do I have enough sample for this read?
exploits.chk4: Does my adjustment do the OPPOSITE of their mistake?
exploits.chk5: If it's a good reg (no clear mistake), do I go back to GTO?
exploits.drill_sub: Choose the right adjustment against station, nit and aggressor. Each correct answer is worth XP.
exploits.t1: GTO is the floor; exploit is the ceiling: deviate to punish the villain's mistake.
exploits.t2: Vs station: zero bluffs, thin and bigger value.
exploits.t3: Vs nit: bluff and steal more, but respect their bets.
exploits.t4: Vs aggressor: call wider; with no sample or vs a good reg, go back to GTO.
exploits.g1_def: Deviating from balance to profit from the villain's specific mistake.
exploits.g2_def: A player who calls too much and hardly ever folds.
exploits.g3_def: A super tight player who folds too much.
exploits.g4_def: A very aggressive player who bluffs and bets too much.
exploits.g5_def: A medium hand used to call the bets of someone who bluffs a lot.
exploits.g6_def: A pattern of mistakes typical of a type of player.
exploits.gl_intro: The theory above turns into practice inside the platform. GrindLab's opponent HUD classifies villains and suggests the adjustment.
exploits.gl1: Classifies each opponent into an archetype (station, nit, LAG) by their stats.
exploits.gl2: Only shows the read with enough sample (no labeling off 5 hands).
exploits.gl3: Suggests the right exploit note per profile (don't bluff a station, etc.).
exploits.gl4: Flags when you bluffed a station or folded too much to a maniac.
exploits.gl5: Generates exploit drills from your own spots against each archetype.

pko.title: PKO tournaments: hunt bounties without losing your way
pko.subtitle: In bounties, every player is worth cash right away. Learn to decide when the reward is worth the risk.
pko.intro_p1: In a normal tournament, you only win money if you reach the payout zone. In a PKO (Progressive Knockout), half the buy-in becomes prize money, and the other half becomes a bounty: a reward you pocket the moment you eliminate someone. Part of that bounty goes straight into your pocket, part sticks to your own head and raises your value as a knockout.
pko.intro_p2: This creates a second game layered on top of the tournament. Beyond trying to survive, you are hunting rewards. And hunting rewards costs chips, so the central question in a PKO is always the same: is this bounty worth the risk I am taking?
pko.intro_note: Players who treat a PKO like an ordinary tournament leave money on the table in the early stages and burn chips chasing bounties they cannot capture. Both mistakes are expensive.
pko.obj1: Understand who actually captures the bounty in a knockout
pko.obj2: Know when the bounty justifies calling wider than usual
pko.obj3: Adjust your aggression according to the tournament stage
pko.obj4: Recognize when ICM starts to weigh more than the reward again
pko.fund_title: The rule that changes everything: only the player who covers captures
pko.fund_p1: The most misunderstood point in a PKO is this: the bounty goes to whoever covers the eliminated player, meaning whoever had the bigger stack in the confrontation. If you have 7bb and go all-in against someone with 40bb, you might double up, but if you lose you are the one who busts, and the villain takes your head. You never capture a bounty from someone who has more chips than you.
pko.fund_p2: Practical consequence: the value of the bounty belongs mostly to the big stacks. They cover almost everyone, so almost every knockout at the table is a potential prize for them. The short stack rarely captures, because almost everyone covers it.
pko.tbl_r1_c: You cover: you have more chips in the confrontation
pko.tbl_r2_a: You (7bb) go all-in and beat a big stack (40bb)
pko.tbl_r2_c: You double up, but you eliminate no one
pko.tbl_r3_c: If the all-in covers the villain, the head is yours
pko.c1_title: When the bounty widens your call
pko.c1_p1: If you cover the villain who went all-in, capturing the bounty is extra money added to the pot. That extra value improves your effective odds, so you can call wider than you would in an ordinary tournament. The bigger the bounty relative to the stack at play, the wider you can go. A hand that would be a tight fold in a normal tournament becomes an easy call when there is a head on the line.
pko.c1_cap: You cover the short stack who shoved. Their bounty is yours if you win, so widen the call.
pko.c1_key: Do you cover the villain and is there a bounty in play? Treat the reward as extra chips in the pot and call wider.
pko.c2_title: When the bounty does nothing for you
pko.c2_p1: Flip the table: if you are the short stack and go all-in against someone who covers you, there is no bounty for you to capture. You are risking your tournament to win only the pot, exactly as in a normal tournament. In that case, ignore the euphoria of the bounties and go back to playing your standard shove for chips and survival.
pko.c2_cap: You are the short stack: you cover no one here. The bounty does not count for you.
pko.c2_warn: Shoving too wide as a short stack because 'there is a lot of bounty at the table'. If you cover no one, those heads are not yours. You are only risking busting out.
pko.c3_title: Stage adjustment: hunt early, tighten near the money
pko.c3_p1: Early in the tournament, bounties are worth a lot relative to the stacks and there is still no payout pressure. This is the time to loosen up: accept marginal confrontations when you cover, because the reward pays for the risk. Near the bubble and the final table, ICM takes over again: survival is worth a lot, and the value of not busting outweighs that of one more head. There you tighten up again.
pko.c3_tip: Early on, hunt bounties (loosen up when you cover). Near the money, respect ICM (tighten up). A PKO is not aggressive all the time.
pko.c4_title: A big bounty on an opponent's head
pko.c4_p1: When a player has piled up many heads, their bounty grows huge, and eliminating them becomes a disproportionate prize. It is worth getting into more confrontations against that target, as long as you cover them. At the same time, be careful: that same player tends to be hunted by everyone, which changes the dynamic of the whole table around them.
pko.c4_coach: Before calling because of the reward, ask yourself: do I cover? What is the real size of the bounty versus my risk? A small head does not justify a bad call.
pko.ex_title: Putting it all together: the decision in three questions
pko.ex_p1: Every all-in decision in a PKO fits into three quick questions. First: do I cover the villain? If not, play it like a normal tournament. Second: how big is the bounty versus the pot and my stack? The bigger it is, the wider I go. Third: what stage am I in? Early I loosen up, near the money I respect ICM.
pko.ex_p2: Notice that the first question alone eliminates half of all PKO mistakes. Most losses come from people risking their tournament for bounties they could never capture.
pko.cmp_title: How each level plays a PKO
pko.cmp_r1_rec: Thinks winning the hand is enough
pko.cmp_r2_pro: Sizes it by the size of the bounty
pko.cmp_r3_rec: Plays the same all tournament long
pko.cmp_r3_pro: Loosens up early and tightens with ICM
pko.err_title: Mistakes that cost you the tournament
pko.e1_m: Shoving wide as a short stack 'because there is bounty'
pko.e1_why: If you cover no one, there is no head for you to capture
pko.e1_fix: As a short stack, play your standard shove for chips
pko.e2_m: Calling tight when you cover and there is a big bounty
pko.e2_why: The reward is extra value that improves your odds
pko.e2_fix: Widen the call in proportion to the size of the bounty
pko.e3_m: Hunting heads on the bubble while ignoring ICM
pko.e3_why: Near the money, survival is worth more than one head
pko.e3_fix: Tighten up near the payouts, respect ICM
pko.e4_m: Playing passive early, not hunting bounties
pko.e4_why: Early on the bounty is huge versus the stack and there is no ICM
pko.e4_fix: Loosen up early when you cover the villain
pko.e5_m: Ignoring the real size of the head in play
pko.e5_why: A small head does not justify a bad call
pko.e5_fix: Compare bounty versus risk before deciding
pko.chk_title: Before calling for a bounty, check
pko.chk1: Do I cover the villain in this confrontation?
pko.chk2: How big is the bounty versus the pot and my stack?
pko.chk3: What stage of the tournament am I in?
pko.chk4: Is ICM already weighing in (bubble, final table)?
pko.chk5: Am I hunting for value or just out of euphoria?
pko.drill_sub: Decide who captures, when to widen the call and how to adjust by stage.
pko.t1: Only the player who covers the eliminated captures the bounty
pko.t2: Cover and there is a head? Call wider, it is extra value
pko.t3: Loosen up early to hunt, tighten up near the money
pko.t4: As a short stack, the bounties at the table are not yours
pko.g1_def: Cash reward you pocket when you eliminate a player.
pko.g2_def: Having more chips than the villain in the confrontation, the condition for capturing their bounty.
pko.g3_def: Format in which part of the bounty captured sticks to your own head.
pko.g4_def: How much the reward in play justifies widening your call.
pko.g5_def: The value of surviving in the tournament, which takes over again near the money.

imbalances.title: The 5 imbalances that define a decision
imbalances.subtitle: Behind every good play there are five axes. Those who see them stop memorizing and start understanding.
imbalances.intro_p1: When a strong player looks at a spot, they do not think 'what was the right play last time'. They weigh a few axes that describe the tension of that moment: how much to bet, how much to protect, how to build the range, how the villain reacts, and which boards favor me. Those axes are the five imbalances.
imbalances.intro_p2: We call them imbalances because each one measures one side pulling against the other. Understanding which side the spot leans toward is what turns rote memory into a real decision. In this lesson you will meet all five and learn to read them at the table.
imbalances.intro_note: On their own, each concept feels abstract. Together they become a mental checklist that covers almost every postflop decision: sizing, aggression, range construction, and villain reads.
imbalances.obj1: Recognize the five axes that describe any spot
imbalances.obj2: Tell a polarized range apart from a condensed range
imbalances.obj3: Adjust your bet sizing to the villain's elasticity
imbalances.obj4: Understand why board coverage protects your range
imbalances.obj5: Use the five as a mental checklist before you act
imbalances.fund_title: The five axes, in one table
imbalances.fund_p1: Before breaking each one down, look at the full map. Each row is a question you ask the spot. The answer tells you which way to lean.
imbalances.tbl_r1_b: How much of my range do I bet, and how often?
imbalances.tbl_r2_b: Do I need to protect my hand or can I go slow?
imbalances.tbl_r3_b: Is my betting range polarized or condensed?
imbalances.tbl_r4_b: Does the villain's call change with the bet size?
imbalances.tbl_r5_b: Do I have value across many types of flop?
imbalances.c1_title: 1 and 2: bet volume and equity management
imbalances.c1_p1: Bet volume is how much of your range turns into a bet and how often. Ranges that bet a lot and big apply more pressure, but they become vulnerable to a raise. Equity management is the other side: hands that need protection (medium pairs on a wet board) prefer to bet to deny equity to the villain, while locked hands can go slow. The two axes clash: betting too much for protection can inflate the pot with a hand that does not want a big pot.
imbalances.c1_key: Bet when protection and value pay for the swelling pot. Go slow when your hand prefers a small pot or is already defined.
imbalances.c2_p1: A polarized range is made of the extremes: very strong hands (value) and bluffs, with little in the middle. A condensed range (or linear) is full of medium and good hands, without so many bluffs or so many monsters. The rule of thumb: polarity calls for a big size (you want to maximize value and give the bluff bad odds), while condensed ranges prefer smaller sizes.
imbalances.c2_tip: If your bet only has the top of your range and bluffs, it is polarized: use a big size. If it has a lot of medium hands, condense the sizing.
imbalances.c3_title: 4: elasticity, reading the villain by the call
imbalances.c3_p1: Elasticity measures how much the villain's call changes with the size of your bet. An elastic villain calls small but folds to big, so against them you tune the sizing to your hand. An inelastic villain (the classic station) calls almost any size, so you bet bigger for value and smaller with bluffs (there is no point bluffing big against someone who never gives up). Elasticity is where theory turns into an exploit.
imbalances.c3_key: Inelastic villain (calls everything): bet bigger for value, cut the bluff. Elastic villain: size by the strength of your hand.
imbalances.c4_p1: Board coverage means having strong hands spread across many types of flop. If your range is only strong on low boards, you are left uncovered when a high flop comes, and a good villain exploits that by betting every time the board works against you. Well built ranges cover high, low, connected, and dry boards, which keeps you able to continue on any texture without becoming an easy target.
imbalances.c4_coach: When you fold too much on a certain type of board, it is a sign of weak coverage there. The fix is rarely 'bluff more now', it is building the range better earlier.
imbalances.c5_p1: In practice, the axes combine. An example: on a dry, high board, your range is usually condensed (few natural bluffs), so you bet small and often, backed by good coverage. On a wet board where the nuts is possible, you polarize, bet big for protection and value, and adjust for how elastic the villain is. No single axis decides on its own.
imbalances.c5_warn: Do not force one axis while ignoring the others. Betting big 'because it is polarized' against an inelastic station is right on value, but the big bluff there is pure money burned.
imbalances.ex_p1: Before acting, run the five in silence: how much to bet (volume), do I need to protect? (equity), is my range polarized? (polarity), does the villain react to the size? (elasticity), do I have value on this board? (coverage). In seconds you have a grounded decision, not a guess.
imbalances.ex_p2: With practice, this stops being a list and becomes intuition. That intuition is exactly what separates those who understand from those who just repeat memorized lines.
imbalances.cmp_title: How each level thinks about the spot
imbalances.cmp_r1_reg: Big with value, small with bluffs
imbalances.cmp_r1_pro: By the axis of the spot (polarity + elasticity)
imbalances.cmp_r2_pro: Measures elasticity and adjusts value and bluff
imbalances.err_title: Mistakes that expose a lack of theory
imbalances.e1_m: Using the same sizing on every board
imbalances.e1_fix: Let the axis of the spot pick the size
imbalances.e2_why: The villain is inelastic, they do not fold to size
imbalances.e2_fix: Bet big for value, cut the bluff
imbalances.e3_m: Betting a medium range with polarized sizing
imbalances.e3_why: A condensed range does not want a big size
imbalances.e3_fix: Condense the sizing when the range is linear
imbalances.e4_m: Always folding on a certain type of board
imbalances.e4_why: It is a symptom of weak coverage there
imbalances.e4_fix: Rebuild the range to cover that texture
imbalances.e5_why: Vulnerable hands hand free equity to the villain
imbalances.e5_fix: Bet to deny equity when the hand calls for it
imbalances.chk_title: Before you act, run the five

imbalances.chk2: Equity: do I need to protect this hand?
imbalances.chk3: Polarity: is my range polarized or condensed?

imbalances.t4: Board coverage is having value across many types of flop
imbalances.t5: The five work together, none decides on its own

imbalances.g2_def: Full of medium and good hands, without so many extremes.

imbalances.g4_def: A villain who calls almost any size, the classic station.

pushfold.title: Short stack: time to shove or fold
pushfold.subtitle: Below ~12bb, almost every preflop decision is binary. Mastering this is what saves you the most chips in MTT.
pushfold.intro_p1: Every tournament, sooner or later, leaves you short. The blinds go up, you don't connect with any hands, and suddenly your stack is at 8, 6, 5 big blinds. At that depth poker changes its nature: you can no longer open small, see a flop, and decide at your leisure. Practically every preflop decision boils down to one thing: all-in or fold.
pushfold.intro_p2: That sounds simple, and it is precisely because it looks simple that so many people get it wrong. Shoving the wrong hand, folding a hand that should go in, or worse, trying a min-raise that doesn't fit the stack. The platform's own data shows that the short stack is one of the biggest sources of error. This lesson fixes that.
pushfold.intro_note: A mistake at 6bb costs proportionally far more than the same mistake at 50bb: you have few chips and each one is your life in the tournament. Getting push/fold right is big money in the long run.
pushfold.obj1: Recognizing when the stack calls for a shove or a fold (and the min-raise leaves the stage)
pushfold.obj2: Understanding why shoving beats min-raising on a short stack
pushfold.obj3: Adjusting how wide you shove according to position
pushfold.obj4: Knowing when to call another player's all-in
pushfold.fund_title: The min-raise disappears, only shove or fold remains
pushfold.fund_p1: On a deep stack, you open 2.2bb and still have plenty behind to play the flop, the turn, the river. On a short stack that vanishes: if you open 2bb with a 6bb stack, so little is left that any resistance already commits you, and you have no chips to bluff with later. The raise becomes a trap, you invest and can neither continue nor give up with any dignity.
pushfold.fund_p2: That is why, below ~12bb, the efficient play collapses into two options: shove (go all-in at once) or fold. The shove resolves the hand immediately and also gives you fold equity, the chance to take the blinds and antes uncontested, because the villain has to risk his entire tournament to call you.
pushfold.fund_key: Below ~12bb: shove or fold. If the hand is good enough to play, it goes all-in. If not, fold it. There is no comfortable middle ground.
pushfold.c1_title: Why shove instead of opening small
pushfold.c1_p1: Shoving is not desperation, it is math. With a short stack, the all-in maximizes fold equity: the villain only calls you by risking his exit from the tournament, so he gives up a lot. You win the blinds and antes right away, without needing to hit the flop. If they call, you still have your hand's equity at showdown. Opening small throws away that pressure: it gives the blinds a cheap price to defend and leaves you with no plan for the following streets.
pushfold.c1_cap: 6bb on the button, everyone folded. A9o is an easy shove: plenty of fold equity and a decent hand if they call.
pushfold.c1_tip: The shove wins in two ways: everyone folds (you take the blinds) or someone calls and your hand competes. Opening small doesn't give you the first one.
pushfold.c2_title: Position dictates how wide you shove
pushfold.c2_p1: Here is the mistake that shows up most: thinking a short stack means shoving almost anything. It doesn't. What decides how wide you shove is how many players are still left to act behind you. The more people behind, the greater the chance someone wakes up with a strong hand and calls you, so the tighter you need to be.
pushfold.tbl_r1_c: Tight: pairs, strong Ax, strong broadways
pushfold.tbl_r2_c: Medium: more Ax, Kx, suited connectors come in
pushfold.tbl_r3_c: Wide: lots of fold equity, shove even weak hands
pushfold.c2_p2: Notice the contrast: the SAME hand can be a shove on the button and a fold from UTG, with the same stack. It is not the hand deciding on its own, it is the hand times the position.
pushfold.c2_cap: 7bb from UTG with the whole table behind. K9o looks playable, but from here it's a fold: too many people left to wake up with a better hand.
pushfold.c2_warn: Shoving way too wide from early position just because you 'are short'. From UTG, with everyone behind, fold equity is low and you get called by the hands that beat you. Short from UTG stays tight.
pushfold.c3_title: Fold equity and depth: the shorter you are, the wider you go
pushfold.c3_p1: Within the same position, how wide you shove also grows as the stack gets shorter. At 10bb, the villain calls you with a more selective range (risking 10bb hurts). At 4bb, your shove is small relative to the pot of blinds and antes, so even weak hands become a profitable shove, and at the same time the villain calls you wider because the price is good. Both effects coexist: you widen your range, but don't expect much fold equity when you are very short.
pushfold.c3_tip: Shorter = wider shoving range (within the same position). But the shorter you are, the less fold equity, so lean more on your hand's equity at showdown.
pushfold.c4_title: The other side: when to call a shove
pushfold.c4_p1: Calling an all-in is a different decision from shoving. There is no fold equity here, you go straight to the confrontation. The rule of thumb is your hand's equity against the entire range the villain shoves: if you beat more than half of that range, plus the cheap price of the pot, the call is profitable. Beware of the 'I have an Ace' trap: a weak Ace (like A4o) loses to a good chunk of a shoving range already full of pairs and better Ax. Calling requires a stronger hand than shoving.
pushfold.c4_coach: To shove, you bet on fold equity plus equity. To call, only on equity. That is why the calling range is always tighter than the shoving range. Don't call with the same criteria you shove.
pushfold.ex_p1: When you are short, run three quick questions. First: what is my stack? Below ~12bb it's shove or fold, forget the min-raise. Second: what is my position? The later I am, the wider I shove; from UTG I stay tight. Third, if I am the one facing a shove: does my hand beat more than half of his range? If yes, I call; if not, I fold.
pushfold.ex_p2: That's all it is. It's not guesswork or courage, it's stack times position, plus an equity calculation when you are the one calling. Automate these three questions and the short stack stops being your biggest source of error.
pushfold.cmp_title: How each level plays the short stack
pushfold.cmp_r1_pro: Shove or fold adjusted for position
pushfold.cmp_r3_pro: Equity vs the entire shoving range
pushfold.e1_why: Commits the stack with no plan for the following streets
pushfold.e2_m: Shoving wide from UTG because you're short
pushfold.e2_why: Whole table behind = low fold equity, you get called by what beats you
pushfold.e2_fix: Short from UTG stays tight; widen in the late positions
pushfold.e3_m: Folding too much from a short button
pushfold.e3_why: Few people behind = a lot of fold equity wasted
pushfold.e3_fix: On a short button/SB, shove much wider
pushfold.e4_why: A weak Ace loses to a good chunk of the shoving range
pushfold.e4_fix: Call only if you beat more than half of his range
pushfold.e5_m: Using the same range to shove and to call
pushfold.e5_why: Calling has no fold equity, it requires a stronger hand
pushfold.e5_fix: The calling range is always tighter than the shoving range
pushfold.chk_title: Before acting with a short stack, check
pushfold.chk1: Is my stack below ~12bb? (then it's shove or fold)
pushfold.chk2: What is my position? How many are left to act behind?
pushfold.chk3: Is the hand good enough to go all-in from here?
pushfold.chk4: If I face a shove: do I beat more than half of his range?
pushfold.chk5: Am I trying a min-raise that doesn't fit the stack?
pushfold.drill_sub: Decide the action, adjust for position, and work out when to call an all-in.
pushfold.t1: Below ~12bb it's shove or fold, the min-raise disappears
pushfold.t2: Shove wins by fold equity plus equity; opening small loses the first one
pushfold.t3: Position rules: UTG tight, button wide
pushfold.t4: Calling all-in requires a stronger hand than shoving
pushfold.g1_def: Going all-in preflop, betting your whole stack at once.
pushfold.g2_def: The value that comes from the chance the villain folds to your shove.
pushfold.g3_def: The binary mode of the short stack: only shove or fold, no middle ground.
pushfold.g4_def: The set of hands you go all-in with from a given position and stack.
pushfold.g5_def: Your stack divided by the cost of one orbit; it measures how short you are.
pushfold.gl_intro: When analyzing your short stack hands, GrindLab looks beyond the isolated hand:
pushfold.gl1: It recognizes the push/fold zone by the depth of the stack
pushfold.gl2: It evaluates the decision as shove or fold, not as a normal open
pushfold.gl3: It considers position and who is left to act behind in the verdict
pushfold.gl4: It treats calling an all-in by the equity rule, not by sizing
pushfold.next_sub: Near the money, the value of surviving changes even short stack decisions.
